dishes.name: Phở
dishes.lit: the rice-noodle itself; name origin debated
dishes.desc: Clear beef or chicken broth over flat rice noodles with herbs; the national dish.

variants.name: phở tái
variants.note: rare beef, sliced thin and cooked by the broth

variants.name: phở chín
variants.note: well-done brisket, no pink

variants.name: phở tái nạm
variants.note: rare beef + flank cut

variants.name: phở gầu
variants.note: fatty brisket — rich, soft fat

variants.name: phở gân
variants.note: beef tendon

variants.name: phở bò viên
variants.note: bouncy beef meatballs

variants.name: phở gà
variants.note: chicken instead of beef, lighter broth

dishes.history:
Born in the Red River Delta around 1900–1910 (Nam Định and Hanoi both claim it), likely where French demand for beef met Chinese noodle-hawker culture; even the name is debated (French 'feu' vs Cantonese 'phấn'). The 1954 partition carried it south, where it gained sweetness and the herb plate; post-1975 refugees carried it worldwide. Today it is Vietnam's culinary ambassador and arguably the country's most famous word abroad.

The dish's birth certificate is usually placed in [Nam Định](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nam_%C4%90%E1%BB%8Bnh), a mill town southeast of Hanoi, where cattle were slaughtered to feed French appetites during the colonial era. Vietnamese cooks inherited the bones the French didn't want — and bones plus rice noodles plus a Chinese hawker tradition equals phở. The word itself may come from 'pot-au-feu', the French boiled-beef dish, or from 'phấn', the Cantonese word for flat rice noodle; a century later, linguists still fight about it at conferences.

The soup's history tracks Vietnam's own. After the 1954 partition, a million northerners moved south and took phở with them; Saigon promptly 'improved' it with sugar, bean sprouts and a garden of herbs, which Hanoi considers vandalism to this day. Ordering phở in Hanoi with hoisin sauce marks you as a southerner or a tourist. After 1975, refugees carried it across the ocean, and the phở shops of Orange County, Sydney and Paris became the first Vietnamese word millions of foreigners ever pronounced.

Fun fact: Hanoi has a dedicated phở vocabulary — 'phở không người lái' ('pilotless phở', meatless) dates from wartime rationing, when the joke was that the bowl, like the American reconnaissance drones overhead, flew without a crew. The dish even has its own national day in Vietnam, December 12.

dishes.name: Bún bò Huế
dishes.desc: Lemongrass-shrimp-paste beef broth, thick noodles, shank, pork knuckle, blood cubes; chili sate on the table, Huế shops run hotter. Stalls sell topping variants under one menu: bún giò (knuckle), bún bò giò (beef + knuckle)…

variants.name: bún giò / bún giò heo
variants.note: pork knuckle or trotter instead of beef — expect a whole chunk of pig foot, skin and tendon on the bone

variants.name: bún giò bò / bún bò giò
variants.note: beef slices + pork knuckle together

variants.name: bún bò tái
variants.note: rare beef, thin-sliced raw and cooked by the hot broth

variants.name: bún gân
variants.note: beef tendon — soft-chewy, gelatinous

variants.name: bún chả cua
variants.note: steamed crab-and-pork sausage balls

variants.name: bún đặc biệt
variants.note: 'special' — a bit of everything, biggest bowl

dishes.history:
Huế was the seat of the [Nguyễn dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_dynasty) for 143 years, and its food culture split in two: fussy, miniature royal cuisine inside the citadel walls, and intense working-class cooking outside them. Bún bò Huế is the rare dish that belongs to both. Legend credits a woman remembered only as Bà Bún — 'Mrs. Noodle' — from the Vân Cù noodle-making village, whose descendants still steam rice vermicelli on the Perfume River's banks.

The broth is the engineering marvel: beef bones and pork bones simmered together, perfumed with a fistful of bruised lemongrass, colored with annatto-chili oil, and anchored by a spoon of mắm ruốc — Huế's fermented shrimp paste — added at exactly the right moment so it deepens the soup without stinking it up. Get that timing wrong and the whole pot is ruined, which is why Huế grandmothers guard it like a state secret.

When [Anthony Bourdain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Bourdain) filmed in Huế in 2014, he called bún bò Huế 'the greatest soup in the world' over a dawn bowl at Đông Ba market, and the city has never let anyone forget it. In Da Nang, the local adaptation is slightly sweeter and the mắm ruốc gentler — Huế people notice, and complain.

dishes.name: Bún chả cá
dishes.desc: Sweet-sour tomato-pineapple broth, fried and poached fish cakes, pickled shallots. Da Nang staple.
dishes.history:
The signature breakfast of Da Nang's fishing economy: daily catch that didn't sell whole was pounded into chả cá, and the frames went into the pot. The tomato-pineapple sourness is the central-coast answer to richer northern fish soups. Locals judge a shop by the bounce of its fish cake.

Every fishing city on Vietnam's central coast pounds its surplus catch into chả cá, but Da Nang turned the habit into a civic institution. The classic shops cluster around Nguyễn Chí Thanh and Lê Hồng Phong streets, many run by the same families for two or three generations, pots the size of oil drums going from 5am. The fish cake comes two ways in one bowl: golden fried slabs and pale poached quenelles, both made from lẹp fish, mackerel or whatever the boats brought in overnight.

What makes the Da Nang bowl unmistakable is the broth's sweet-sour register — tomatoes, pineapple and sometimes a little tamarind, lightened further by pickled shallots and a mountain of shredded cabbage on the side. It's engineered for the tropics: where Hanoi soups warm you up, bún chả cá cools you down, which is why locals eat it for breakfast before the heat arrives.

The dish also explains the local economy in miniature: [Da Nang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Da_Nang) grew as a fishing port long before it grew as a beach resort, and the morning chả cá routine — boats in at 4, cakes pounded by 5, bowls out by 6 — still runs on fishing-fleet time, not tourist time.

dishes.name: Mì Quảng
dishes.lit: Quảng noodles — rice, not wheat, despite the name
dishes.desc: Wide turmeric noodles in scant intense broth, pork, shrimp, peanuts, sesame rice cracker.

variants.name: mì Quảng tôm thịt
variants.note: the classic: shrimp + pork belly

variants.name: mì Quảng gà
variants.note: chicken, often free-range with bone pieces

variants.name: mì Quảng cá lóc
variants.note: snakehead fish

variants.name: mì Quảng ếch
variants.note: frog — a Da Nang favourite

variants.name: mì Quảng đặc biệt
variants.note: 'special' — mixed toppings, usually all of the above minus fish/frog

dishes.history:
Quảng Nam's celebration dish — the scant broth is deliberate, so trays could sit through village gatherings without going soggy. The name says 'mì' (wheat noodle, a Chinese-trader echo) though the noodle is rice. In 2024 it was inscribed as Vietnamese national intangible cultural heritage, and for Quảng people it remains the taste of home more than any restaurant dish.

Mì Quảng is [Quảng Nam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qu%E1%BA%A3ng_Nam_province) province's autobiography in a bowl. The wide rice noodles get their yellow from turmeric (historically, some villages used gardenia seeds), the broth is deliberately scant — more a concentrated sauce than a soup — and the toppings run to whatever the land gave: shrimp and pork on the coast, chicken inland, frog in the paddies, snakehead fish along the rivers. The crown is always the same: roasted peanuts, fresh herbs, and a shard of toasted sesame rice cracker you crumble in for crunch.

Food historians read the dry format as party logistics. Quảng Nam villages cooked mì Quảng for weddings, harvest festivals and death anniversaries, where trays had to sit out for hours; a brothy noodle would die, a scant-sauce noodle survives. It's also why every family claims their version is 'đúng vị' — the true taste — and why the dish tolerates infinite variation without losing its name.

In 2024 mì Quảng was inscribed on Vietnam's list of [national intangible cultural heritage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage), alongside Hanoi phở. For the Quảng diaspora it functions like a passport: the first thing they eat coming home, the dish they measure every other region's cooking against, usually unfavorably.

dishes.name: Cao lầu
dishes.desc: Hội An only: chewy ash-treated noodles, char-siu pork, crispy croutons, minimal broth.
dishes.history:
Hội An's port history in a bowl: noodles laced with lye-ash (traditionally from Chàm Islands wood, mixed with Bá Lễ well water), char-siu-style pork from Chinese merchants, and a texture travellers compare to Japanese udon — all three trading communities left fingerprints. It resists export: outside Hội An the water and ash are 'wrong', which is exactly the town's point of pride.

Cao lầu is the dish equivalent of [Hội An](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%E1%BB%99i_An) itself: a 16th–18th century trading port where Japanese, Chinese and Vietnamese merchants lived in adjacent streets, preserved in amber. The noodles are the mystery — thick, chewy, tea-colored, treated with lye made from wood ash, which tradition insists must come from the Chàm Islands offshore, mixed with water from the ancient Bá Lễ well. Everyone compares them to udon; nobody has proven the Japanese connection, but the Japanese Covered Bridge is a five-minute walk from the noodle stalls, so the theory writes itself.

The pork on top is xá xíu — Cantonese char siu wearing Vietnamese fish-sauce clothes — and the croutons are fried squares of the same noodle dough, a zero-waste trick as old as the recipe. Underneath sits a handful of greens from Trà Quế, the herb village just outside town whose beds have been fertilized with river algae for centuries.

The name likely means 'high floor': merchants ate it upstairs in the shophouses, above the flood line and the street noise, keeping an eye on their warehouses. It remains almost impossible to reproduce elsewhere — Saigon restaurants import the noodles from Hội An daily, and Hội An people will tell you even that doesn't really work.

dishes.name: Bún riêu
dishes.desc: Tangy tomato broth with freshwater-crab-and-egg clouds, tofu, sometimes snails.
dishes.history:
A masterpiece of paddy-field frugality: tiny rice-field crabs, pounded whole and strained, curdle into the protein 'clouds' that crown the bowl. Once peasant food of the northern delta, it now has cult status nationwide, and the crab-tomato sour register defines a whole family of Vietnamese soups.

Bún riêu is what happens when a rice-farming civilization refuses to waste protein. The riêu — those russet clouds floating on the tomato broth — is made from rice-paddy crabs the size of a coin, pounded whole in a mortar, strained, and coaxed into curds in the simmering pot. It took the cheapest protein in the northern delta and made it luxurious; the crab roe stirred with egg is what binds the raft together.

The bowl is a study in sourness. Tomatoes give the base, but the finishing acid is traditionally giấm bỗng, a fermented rice vinegar with a faint sweetness that's hard to find outside the north; southern versions lean on tamarind instead and add more sugar, as southern versions do. A dollop of mắm tôm (purple shrimp paste) at the table is orthodox — skip it and a Hanoi vendor will assume you're unwell.

Bún riêu also anchors a whole family of related bowls — riêu cá (fish), riêu ốc (snail, a Hanoi specialty), and hybrid crab-snail-beef versions that southern markets pile high. Anthony Bourdain ate bún riêu on camera in Saigon and called it one of his favorite bowls in Vietnam, which briefly made the humble field crab a celebrity.

dishes.name: Bún mắm
dishes.desc: Pungent dark fermented-fish broth with pork, shrimp, squid, eggplant. Mekong style.
dishes.history:
The Mekong's boldest soup, built on mắm cá linh/sặc — fermented river fish inherited from Khmer prahok culture. It was fisherfolk preservation economics before it was cuisine; today it's often called Vietnam's 'gumbo of the delta'. The funk is the point: locals say if it smells polite, it's wrong.

Bún mắm begins with prahok logic: the [Mekong Delta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mekong_Delta) floods every year, the flood brings a biblical quantity of small fish (cá linh above all), and the only way to keep protein through the dry season is to salt and ferment it. The Khmer, who lived in the delta first, did exactly that; Vietnamese settlers adopted the technique, and mắm — fermented freshwater fish — became the region's pantry, currency and seasoning at once.

The soup dissolves that fermented fish into the broth itself, which is why there's no hiding from it: dark, savory, sweet underneath, with a scent that announces the pot from across the market. Onto that go pork belly, shrimp, squid, and the delta's signature vegetable, roasted eggplant, plus a raw-vegetable plate that can include water lily stems and banana blossom.

Sóc Trăng and Cần Thơ both claim the definitive version; Saigon's Bến Thành-area shops built the dish's city reputation. Foreign food writers reliably call it 'Vietnamese gumbo' — the comparison is lazy but not wrong: both are dark, swampy, everything-in-the-pot soups from a river delta with a layered colonial history.

dishes.name: Bún cá
dishes.desc: Lighter cousin of bún chả cá — fresh fish slices in a clean, dilly-sour broth.
dishes.history:
The everyday fish noodle of the central coast — lighter and cleaner than chả cá versions, historically the way port towns from Quy Nhơn to Nha Trang stretched the morning catch. Each town's version (dill here, pineapple there) is a small map of local fishing culture.

Bún cá is less one dish than a coastline's worth of local answers to the same question: what do you do with this morning's fish? Hải Phòng makes it brown and brawny with fried fish and dill; Nha Trang adds jellyfish and fish-cake; Quy Nhơn's version is famous enough that shops in Saigon advertise 'bún cá Quy Nhơn' as a brand; Châu Đốc, near the Cambodian border, yellows its broth with turmeric and fermented fish in a way that shades into Khmer cooking.

The central-coast bowls this entry describes keep the broth clean and clear on purpose — fish bones simmered fast, tomatoes and pineapple for brightness, dill or scallion greens on top. It's the lighter sibling of bún chả cá: fresh fillet instead of pounded cake, closer to the sea's own taste.

A detail worth knowing at the stall: the pickled bamboo (măng chua) or pickled shallots served alongside aren't garnish, they're the acid balance the broth is built around. Locals load them in immediately, stir once, and only then taste.

dishes.name: Bánh canh cua
dishes.desc: Gravy-thick broth, fat udon-like tapioca noodles, crab, quail eggs.
dishes.history:
Bánh canh ('soup cake') noodles were a home-kitchen economy — rolled and cut straight into the pot, no drying step. The crab-thickened version rose from working-class carts to restaurant favourite in Huế and Saigon alike; the gravy-thick broth shows the Vietnamese comfort register at its most unapologetic.

Bánh canh translates literally as 'soup cake' — the noodle began as rice or tapioca dough rolled out and sliced straight into boiling broth, no drying stage, the way a farmhouse cook improvises. The tapioca-heavy versions turn translucent and slippery-chewy, and their starch thickens the broth into something closer to gravy, which is exactly the point.

The crab version is the aristocrat of the family. In Huế, bánh canh cua rời shops sell the crab already picked and pressed into rich cakes; in Saigon, bowls come loaded with quail eggs, shrimp and a startling orange slick of crab fat. Street versions cost pocket change; 'premium' bánh canh cua with whole crab claws has become a minor Saigon flex, with bowls costing ten times the ordinary price photographed for social media.

Other branches of the family are their own institutions: bánh canh Trảng Bàng from Tây Ninh province built an entire pilgrimage-town restaurant strip on pork-trotter bánh canh, and coastal towns do bánh canh chả cá as breakfast. The dish is a reminder that 'noodle soup' in Vietnam is not a category but a civilization.

dishes.name: Hủ tiếu Nam Vang
dishes.desc: Clear pork-and-dried-seafood broth with pork, shrimp, quail egg, liver. Saigon icon.
dishes.history:
Nam Vang is the Vietnamese name for Phnom Penh: Teochew Chinese cooks brought this pork-and-seafood noodle from Cambodia to Saigon in the mid-20th century (kuy teav in Khmer). It became the south's aristocrat of breakfast soups and a marker of Chợ Lớn's Chinese-Khmer-Viet triangle.

The name says the route: Nam Vang is Vietnamese for [Phnom Penh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phnom_Penh), and the dish is the Khmer kuy teav as cooked by Teochew Chinese merchants who worked the Mekong trade between Cambodia and Saigon's Chinese twin-city, [Chợ Lớn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%A3_L%E1%BB%9Bn). When ethnic Chinese and Vietnamese left Cambodia in waves through the 1970s, the soup came with them, and Saigon adopted it as its own.

The broth is the tell: pork bones sweetened with dried squid and dried shrimp, clear as consommé, with a faint ocean depth that beef soups don't have. Toppings are a Teochew inventory — sliced pork, minced pork, shrimp, quail egg, and in the full version pork liver, heart and intestine. Garlic oil and pickled garlic vinegar on the table are mandatory equipment.

Ordering has its own grammar: 'nước' gets you soup; 'khô' gets the noodles dressed in dark sweet soy with the broth in a side bowl, which many regulars consider the superior format. In Phnom Penh the ancestor dish is still breakfast royalty — eating both cities' versions back to back is a small history lesson in how food outlives borders.

dishes.name: Miến gà
dishes.desc: Light clear chicken broth with translucent mung-bean noodles.
dishes.history:
The northern answer to 'what do we do with the good chicken broth' — especially after Tết, when boiled offering-chickens needed a second life. Glass noodles kept it light and festive; it remains the soup of family kitchens more than street stalls.

Miến — glass noodles made from mung bean or canna starch — occupy a special shelf in northern cooking: the noodle for occasions when rice noodles feel too heavy or too ordinary. Miến gà is their gentlest use: clear chicken broth, translucent noodle skeins, shredded poached bird, and a confetti of fried shallots and laksa leaf (rau răm).

Its cultural home is the post-feast morning. Northern ritual meals — Tết above all — center on a whole boiled chicken presented to the ancestors; the day after, the carcass and the good broth become miến gà, which is why the dish tastes like the calm after a celebration to most Hanoi families. Street versions add the parts the feast held back: hearts, gizzards, unlaid eggs for the adventurous.

A practical note from the noodle itself: miến keeps its bite in hot broth far longer than rice vermicelli, which made it the traveler's and vendor's friend — and it's the reason the bowl survives delivery apps better than phở does. In the south the same noodle goes into miến gà tiềm and duck versions; the north keeps it austere and considers that the point.

dishes.name: Bánh đa cua
dishes.desc: Hải Phòng's brick-red crab noodle soup with chewy brown noodles, betel-leaf beef rolls.
dishes.history:
Hải Phòng's identity dish, as red as the city's flame-tree symbol: brown-red bánh đa noodles, field crab, and betel-leaf pork rolls. Port-city dockworker food that the city elevated into civic pride — Hải Phòng people abroad speak of it the way Huế people speak of bún bò.

[Hải Phòng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hai_Phong) is Vietnam's gruff northern port — shipyards, coal dust, flame trees — and bánh đa cua is its self-portrait. The noodle is bánh đa đỏ, a wide brown-red rice noodle made in villages like Dư Hàng Kênh, whose color comes from the rice being toasted or mixed with molasses-dark seasonings before pressing. No other city uses it; carrying dried bánh đa đỏ home is what Hải Phòng people do when they emigrate.

The bowl assembles the whole delta-meets-sea economy: field-crab curds like bún riêu's, chả lá lốt (pork grilled in wild betel leaves), fish cake, fried tofu, water spinach and a lick of chili paste. It's bigger and darker than Hanoi's crab soups, befitting a dockworker's breakfast.

Hải Phòng natives are famously loyal to it — the city's food identity (bánh đa cua, nem cua bể, giá bể) is a point of pride against Hanoi's polish. The dish's red noodle even shows up in the city's tourism slogans, twinned with the red flame-tree blossom that gives Hải Phòng its nickname, 'city of the phoenix flowers'.

dishes.name: Bún mọc
dishes.desc: Clear pork broth with bouncy mọc balls (pork paste + wood-ear), ribs, herbs. Gentle Hanoi breakfast.
dishes.history:
Mọc are the point: pork paste pounded with wood-ear mushroom and rolled into balls that bounce — the texture Vietnamese call 'giòn dai' and prize above tenderness. The dish is attributed to the old Mọc village (Nhân Mục) on Hanoi's edge, which gave the balls their name.

It's the mildest member of the northern bún canon — no fermented backbone, no chili wall — which makes it the bowl Hanoi parents order for children and the safe harbor on any soup menu. Office-district shops sell out by mid-morning, often pairing it with sườn sụn (crunchy rib cartilage) for the texture-minded.

dishes.name: Bún măng vịt
dishes.desc: Duck and sour-ish bamboo shoot in clear broth; dipped in gừng-spiked fish sauce. The duck day's classic.
dishes.history:
Duck and bamboo shoot is one of the oldest pairings in the Vietnamese kitchen — the shoot's faint sourness cuts the bird's fat, and the ginger-heavy dipping sauce (nước mắm gừng) is mandatory, not optional. Folk custom even assigns the dish a calendar: duck is eaten to 'wash away' bad luck at the fifth lunar month and month's end.

The bowl is a two-part meal: broth and noodles in one bowl, the chopped duck on a plate with the ginger sauce, plus a gỏi vịt cabbage salad at proper shops. Everything at the table exists to serve the duck-ginger-bamboo triangle.

dishes.name: Bún ốc
dishes.desc: Hanoi's tart tomato-snail soup, soured with fermented rice vinegar, chili oil on top.
dishes.history:
Bún ốc is old Hanoi in a bowl: pond snails, tomatoes, and giấm bỗng — the fermented rice vinegar that gives the broth its signature gentle sourness, a flavor southern kitchens replace with tamarind and Hanoians insist is irreplaceable. The chili here is ớt chưng, oil-fried chili paste, floated on top in rust-red islands.

The dish carries a whole vendor culture: the bún ốc ladies of West Lake and the old quarter, some serving 'bún ốc nguội' — the archaic room-temperature version connoisseurs seek out — from earthenware pots. Writers on Hanoi food (Vũ Bằng, Thạch Lam) treated bún ốc as a benchmark of the city's taste; it remains a dish tourists rarely find and locals never give up.

dishes.name: Bún thang
dishes.desc: Hanoi's most refined bowl: clear chicken-dried-shrimp broth over fine-shredded chicken, egg ribbons, chả lụa.
dishes.history:
Bún thang is Hanoi cooking showing off through restraint: a crystal broth of chicken and dried shrimp, and toppings cut to matchstick fineness — poached chicken, omelet ribbons, chả lụa, all arranged like a color wheel. The name is argued over: 'thang' as in a prescription of many ingredients (the dish uses up to twenty), or as in ladder/steps of layered toppings.

It began as a post-Tết economy — transforming the altar chicken and pantry odds into elegance — and became the benchmark of a Hanoi family's kitchen discipline. The finishing touches are famously fussy: a dab of mắm tôm, and for purists a toothpick's worth of cà cuống, the near-extinct water-beetle essence that perfumes the steam.

dishes.name: Miến lươn
dishes.desc: Glass noodles with eel two ways — crispy-fried or soft-braised — in a dark, peppery eel-bone broth.
dishes.history:
Rice-paddy eel is central Nghệ An's gift to the northern table: small wild lươn, once caught by hand in the mud after harvest, stir-fried with turmeric until crisp or kept soft, over miến in a broth made from the bones. Ordering is binary — 'giòn' (crispy) or 'mềm' (soft) — and shops are judged on doing both well.

Hanoi adopted the dish so thoroughly that miến lươn streets exist (Chân Cầm, Hàng Điếu), and the crispy version doubles as a dry bowl, miến lươn trộn. Bean sprouts, fried shallots and a violent quantity of black pepper are canon; the eel's earthiness is the taste people either chase for life or politely avoid.

dishes.name: Bún bung
dishes.desc: Homely northern bowl: pork ribs and hocks simmered soft with dọc mùng stems, tomato-blushed broth.
dishes.history:
Bún bung is the northern delta's Sunday-pot soup: pork ribs, hock and patties simmered until 'bung' — burst-soft — with dọc mùng, the spongy taro-stem vegetable that drinks up broth like a sponge. Thái Bình province claims the canonical version, sometimes yellowed with turmeric and served with sườn sụn cartilage.

In Hanoi it lives in the shadow of bún riêu and bún ốc, sold by fewer and fewer vendors, which gives it heirloom status — food writers file it under 'bowls your grandmother knew'. It's the mildest possible introduction to the northern habit of building soups on pork bones and garden acidity rather than spice.

dishes.name: Bún thịt nướng
dishes.desc: No broth: cool vermicelli, charcoal pork, herbs, pickles, peanuts, fish-sauce dressing.
dishes.history:
The template of the Vietnamese 'dry bowl': cool vermicelli under smoking-hot charcoal pork, dressed at the table with nước chấm. It made Vietnamese food legible abroad — the 'noodle bowl' of overseas menus is essentially this dish — and at home it's the lunch that survives the hottest months.

The genius of the dry bowl is thermodynamic: cool vermicelli below, charcoal-hot pork above, raw herbs and pickles between, and nước chấm — the sweetened, lime-sharpened fish-sauce dressing — poured over everything at the last second. Nothing wilts, nothing stews; every mouthful is assembled in the chopsticks.

The pork itself carries the signature: thin belly or shoulder slices marinated in fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass and shallots, grilled over coals until the sugar chars at the edges. That caramel-smoke smell drifting down a street at 11am is bún thịt nướng advertising itself. In the south the bowl often adds a broken-up fried spring roll (bún thịt nướng chả giò), which is arguably the best of both worlds in one dish.

Abroad, this bowl quietly became the template for what Western menus call a 'Vietnamese noodle bowl' or 'vermicelli bowl' — the format survived translation better than any soup because it needs no broth mastery, only a grill. At home it stays a lunch dish: quick, cool, eaten fast in the shade.

dishes.name: Bún chả
dishes.desc: Hanoi lunch: grilled patties and belly in sweet-sour fish-sauce broth, noodles for dunking.
dishes.history:
Hanoi's lunchtime institution since at least the French era — alleys announce it by charcoal smoke at 11am sharp. It went global in 2016 when Anthony Bourdain took President Obama for bún chả and beer on plastic stools; the shop preserved their table under glass, and 'bún chả Obama' entered the language.

Bún chả is Hanoi's midday religion. The format is precise: a bowl of warm, sweet-sour-salty dipping broth holding two kinds of pork — chả viên patties seasoned with shallot and pepper, and chả miếng, caramel-edged belly slices — plus a basket of cold vermicelli and a hedge of herbs. You dip, you pull, you slurp; the broth is a sauce, not a soup, and drinking it straight marks the newcomer.

The smoke is the city's clock. Bún chả is grilled over coals in the hour before lunch, and old Hanoi streets like Hàng Mành and Hàng Quạt announce noon by smell. The dish barely exists at dinner — vendors sell out and close, and Hanoians consider evening bún chả slightly unnatural.

Its global moment came on 23 May 2016, when [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama) and Anthony Bourdain shared bún chả and Hanoi beer on blue plastic stools at Bún Chả Hương Liên for a 'Parts Unknown' episode. The shop encased their table and bowls in a glass box, added a 'Combo Obama' to the menu, and the phrase 'bún chả Obama' entered Vietnamese. The episode aired after Bourdain's death gave the scene a second, sadder fame.

dishes.name: Bún đậu mắm tôm
dishes.desc: Platter of vermicelli bricks, fried tofu, boiled pork, dipped in purple fermented shrimp paste.
dishes.history:
A Hanoi sidewalk platter built around mắm tôm, the purple shrimp paste that functions as a cultural initiation rite. Once a between-shifts snack of market porters, it has been reborn as a youth favourite — the 'mẹt' bamboo tray format is now a nationwide franchise phenomenon.

Bún đậu mắm tôm is a dare wrapped in a picnic. The mẹt — a bamboo tray lined with banana leaf — arrives loaded: bricks of pressed vermicelli, golden-fried tofu, boiled pork, chả cốm (pork patties studded with young green rice), cucumber and herbs. In the middle sits the point of the exercise: mắm tôm, fermented shrimp paste the color of amethyst, whipped with lime, sugar and a splash of hot fat until it foams.

The paste divides humanity. To the initiated it's umami dynamite; to everyone else it's a public-order incident. Vietnamese office workers negotiate mắm tôm the way other offices negotiate durian — some buildings informally ban lunchtime bún đậu breath. The polite fallback (asking for fish sauce instead) is tolerated but quietly mourned by the vendor.

The dish began as Hanoi alley food — cheap tofu, cheap noodles, market-porter fuel around Hàng Khay and Phất Lộc lane — and then, in the 2010s, became a youth phenomenon: bún đậu franchises with neon signs now operate across Saigon and even in Vietnamese communities abroad, selling a curated version of the Hanoi sidewalk on a tray.

dishes.name: Mì xào
dishes.desc: Wok-fried wheat noodles, soft or crispy, with beef, seafood or vegetables.
dishes.history:
The Chinese wok arriving in Vietnamese kitchens: egg-wheat noodles stir-fried soft or bird's-nest crispy. It rode Chợ Lớn's cook-shop culture into every rice-plate eatery in the country and remains the default 'safe order' for children and homesick diners alike.

Mì xào is the sound of Chợ Lớn cooking: a seasoned wok, egg noodles, fire under everything. Vietnam absorbed the technique from Cantonese and Teochew cook-shops and then ran it through local taste — fish sauce and oyster sauce sharing the pan, morning glory and gai lan swapping in and out, beef or seafood by mood.

The two canonical formats map to two textures. Mì xào mềm keeps the noodles soft and glossy, tumbled with the toppings; mì xào giòn fries the noodle nest crisp and pours the saucy stir-fry over it, so it crackles, then soaks, then chews — three dishes in one plate if you pace yourself. The crispy version is banquet food demoted happily to street food.

Its social role is the reliable middle: every quán cơm and every late-night street kitchen can produce a mì xào, it never threatens anyone's palate, and it's the dish Vietnamese parents order for tired children. That ubiquity is its history — the wok arrived with migrants centuries ago and simply never left.

dishes.name: Miến trộn
dishes.desc: Glass noodles tossed dry with toppings — crispy eel in Hanoi, shrimp-pork elsewhere — sharp with lime and chili.
dishes.history:
Miến trộn is the dry-bowl logic applied to glass noodles: since miến holds a chewy spring long after rice noodles surrender, it takes a hot toss of fried shallot oil, fish sauce, lime and chili without wilting. Hanoi's definitive version rides on crispy turmeric eel; beach towns toss it with seafood; vegetarian shops build it on tofu and mushrooms for the lunar fast days.

It's a young-ish genre — the trộn (tossed) bowls colonized menus in the 2000s as lighter lunch alternatives — but it inherits an old northern idea: the noodle as texture instrument first, vehicle second.

dishes.name: Mít trộn
dishes.desc: Shredded boiled green jackfruit tossed with pork/shrimp, peanuts, herbs; scooped on rice crackers.
dishes.history:
Da Nang student food: boiled young jackfruit — cheap and everywhere in central gardens — shredded and dressed like a meat salad. It travelled from poverty stretch-dish to beer-hall essential, usually scooped with sesame rice crackers instead of spoons.

Mít trộn is Da Nang proving that a salad can be beer food. Young green jackfruit — plentiful, nearly free, and meatless in texture only — is boiled until tender, shredded into strands that look uncannily like pulled pork, then tossed with pork or shrimp, a heavy hand of roasted peanuts, fried shallots, rau răm and a chili-lime fish sauce that soaks into everything.

The scoop is the ritual: it's eaten with bánh tráng mè, sesame rice crackers, snapped into shards and used as edible spoons. The contrast — hot crunchy cracker, cool spicy-sweet salad — is the entire experience, and a table of students demolishing a plate sounds like a small hailstorm.

The dish belongs to the student-quarter economy around Da Nang's universities, where a plate costs less than a coffee, and to nhậu culture — the Vietnamese institution of drinking sessions built around shared snacks. Elsewhere in the center the same idea appears as nộm mít; Da Nang just claims the loudest version.

dishes.name: Hủ tiếu khô
dishes.desc: Noodles tossed in sweet-savoury sauce, toppings on top, broth on the side.
dishes.history:
The 'khô' — dry — format is southern noodle culture's quiet masterpiece. Take the same Teochew hủ tiếu, but instead of drowning the noodles, dress them: dark soy, sugar, garlic oil, sometimes a spoon of the shop's secret sa tế. The broth arrives anyway, demoted to a side cup with a floating meatball, so you get two temperatures, two textures, one order.

Regulars will tell you khô is the connoisseur's choice because it can't hide anything — a mediocre broth disappears into a soup bowl, but bad noodles or lazy seasoning are naked on a dry plate. The style almost certainly spread through Chợ Lớn's cook-shops, where Teochew 'kway teow' cooking already had dry-tossed traditions.

There's also a rural cousin worth knowing: hủ tiếu Mỹ Tho from the Mekong, whose chewy-clear noodles made the town's name a brand since the 1960s; many Saigon hủ tiếu khô shops advertise Mỹ Tho noodles specifically. Breakfast is the dish's natural hour, eaten fast, with the broth cup as the chaser.

dishes.name: Phở xào
dishes.desc: Wide phở noodles wok-fried with beef and greens in glossy sauce.
dishes.history:
Hanoi's wok remix of its own icon — phở noodles seared with beef and gai lan in glossy sauce. It began as a way for phở shops to use noodles past soup-prime hours and became a late-night genre of its own, beloved after beer.

Phở xào is what Hanoi does with its icon after the lunch rush: the same flat rice noodles, seared in a violently hot wok with beef, garlic and gai lan until the edges blister and catch. Done right, the noodles pick up 'hơi thở của chảo' — the breath of the wok, that faint char Cantonese cooks call wok hei — and the glossy oyster-fish-sauce glaze ties it together.

The dish is a document of Hanoi's Chinese culinary layer: it's essentially beef chow fun naturalized into the phở family, and the best versions still come from old quarter shops that also sell mì vằn thắn (wonton noodles). Phở xào giòn, where the noodle cake is fried crisp and sauced, is the dressier cousin.

Its hour is the night. Hanoi eats phở nước at dawn and phở xào after dark — post-beer, post-cinema, when a soup feels thin and a plate of smoking noodles feels correct. A fried egg on top is a common upgrade and never a mistake.

dishes.name: Cơm tấm
dishes.desc: Broken rice with grilled pork chop, shredded skin, steamed egg cake, fried egg, fish sauce.
dishes.history:
Broken rice was the miller's discard, sold cheap to Saigon dockworkers and rickshaw men; vendors dressed it up with a grilled chop, shredded skin and steamed egg cake until the 'poor man's plate' became the city's defining breakfast. The fracture is now the feature — the small grains hold fish-sauce dressing better than whole rice.

Cơm tấm is Saigon's rice-mill Cinderella story. Tấm is the broken grain sifted out at milling — once sold cheap to dockworkers, rickshaw pullers and students because whole grains fetched better prices. Vendors dressed the broken rice up: a fish-sauce-and-honey-marinated pork chop grilled over coals, bì (shredded pork and skin tossed with roasted rice powder), and chả trứng, a steamed pork-egg loaf. The plate outgrew its poverty and became the city's signature breakfast.

The details are non-negotiable. Broken rice cooks up drier and nubblier than whole rice, which is exactly why it holds the sweet nước chấm without turning to porridge. Scallion oil goes over everything; pickled daikon and carrot cut the fat; the correct utensils are fork and spoon, a colonial-era habit that survived on this one plate more than any other.

Saigon eats cơm tấm at every hour — the after-midnight cơm tấm đêm stalls are an institution of taxi drivers and club crowds — and the smoke from ten thousand pork-chop grills is part of the city's morning air. In 2012, when a famous chain tried trademark-policing the words 'cơm tấm', the city's collective laughter settled the question of who owns the dish.

dishes.name: Cơm gà Hội An
dishes.desc: Turmeric rice cooked in chicken stock, torn poached chicken, herb-onion salad, papaya pickle.
dishes.history:
Hainanese chicken rice, filtered through Hội An's Chinese merchant community and central Vietnamese herbs — turmeric rice cooked in stock, hand-torn hen, onion-herb salad. Mid-century vendors like Bà Buội fixed the canon; today it's the town's second flag after cao lầu.

Cơm gà Hội An is what happened when [Hainanese chicken rice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hainanese_chicken_rice) sailed into a Vietnamese trading port and stayed. Hội An's Chinese merchant families brought the technique — rice cooked in chicken fat and broth — and the town rebuilt it in its own colors: turmeric-gold rice, free-range hens torn by hand into ragged strips, and a sharp salad of onion, Vietnamese coriander and green papaya where Singapore would put cucumber slices.

The canon was fixed by mid-20th-century vendors, most famously Bà Buội, whose shop on Phan Chu Trinh street began as a shoulder-pole stall in the 1950s; 'cơm gà Bà Buội' is now practically a controlled designation, imitated all over town. The other lineage names — Bà Nga, Bà Hương — mark a matriarchy of chicken rice.

Eaten in place, it comes with a bowl of broth fortified with cắc-cắc offal bits, chili jam on the table, and the low light of the old town. The dish is small, cheap and quietly perfect — the culinary equivalent of Hội An's shophouse architecture: borrowed forms, local materials, aged into something that belongs to no one else.

variants.name: cơm gà Tam Kỳ / Quảng Nam
variants.note: the inland Quảng Nam style — more chicken-salad forward, plainer rice; Tam Kỳ people insist it predates Hội An's

dishes.name: Cơm hến
dishes.desc: Huế's thrifty classic: cold rice, stir-fried baby clams, crackling, peanuts, chili; clam broth aside.
dishes.history:
Huế's monument to thrift: cold leftover rice with tiny basket clams dredged from the Cồn Hến islet in the Perfume River, plus a dozen garnishes that cost almost nothing. Once food of the poorest riverbank families, it's now served with pride as the essence of Huế's flavour philosophy — spicy, layered, frugal.

Cơm hến is Huế's monument to making almost nothing into something unforgettable. The hến are basket clams the size of a fingernail, dredged from around Cồn Hến, an islet in the Perfume River whose families held the clamming rights for generations. The clams are boiled, the meat picked, and the cooking water becomes the broth served alongside — cloudy, sweet, tasting of the river.

The bowl itself is an inventory of thrift: cold leftover rice (never fresh — the chew matters), the clam meat stir-fried with mắm ruốc, then crackling pork fat, roasted peanuts, sesame, banana blossom, star fruit, herbs, chili paste. Twenty ingredients, none expensive, layered like sediment. You pour the clam broth over or sip it separately; both schools consider the other wrong.

Huế royals reportedly requested it in the citadel — the poorest dish in the city commanding the richest table — and that inversion is very Huế: a city that seasons austerity until it outclasses luxury. Da Nang does a version with mì hến (noodles), but pilgrims still cross the Cồn Hến bridge for the original, eaten on low stools among the clam sheds.

dishes.name: Cơm niêu
dishes.desc: Rice baked in a clay pot for a golden crust, served with braised dishes.
dishes.history:
Clay-pot rice from the era before rice cookers, prized for the golden cơm cháy crust at the bottom. Saigon restaurants turned it into dinner theatre — pots smashed tableside, rice discs flying between waiters — but the soul of the dish is the crust-and-braise pairing of old family kitchens.

Rice cooked in a clay pot predates every rice cooker and survives them all, because the niêu does something electronics can't: cơm cháy, the golden crust where rice meets hot clay, prized enough that families used to argue over it. Clay breathes, holds heat unevenly on purpose, and rewards attention — tilt the pot, listen for the crackle, pull it at the right minute.

Saigon turned the humble pot into dinner theater in the 1990s: at the famous cơm niêu restaurants, waiters smash the pot with a hammer, liberate the rice as a molded disc, and hurl it across the room to a colleague who catches it one-handed and dresses it with scallion oil and sesame tableside. Anthony Bourdain caught a flying rice cake on camera in 2000, on his first Vietnam trip, and the trick became a tourist rite.

Beyond the spectacle, the real tradition is the table around the pot: village-style braises — cá kho tộ, thịt kho, stir-fried water spinach, canh chua — with the crusty rice as anchor. It's Vietnamese home cooking formalized into a restaurant genre, nostalgia you can eat with a spoon you scraped yourself.

dishes.name: Cơm chiên
dishes.desc: Fried rice; dương châu (Yangzhou) with mixed meats is the café standard.
dishes.history:
Fried rice came with Cantonese cook-shops ('Dương Châu' is Yangzhou), then Vietnam made it its own with fish sauce and lạp xưởng. It is the great leftover-redeemer of home cooking and the default filler dish of every quán in the country.

Fried rice entered Vietnam with Cantonese cooks — 'Dương Châu' fried rice is literally [Yangzhou](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yangzhou) — and Vietnam promptly made it its own: fish sauce in the seasoning, lạp xưởng (sweet Chinese sausage) and char siu among the mix-ins, cà pháo pickles or chili sauce on the side. Every quán in the country can produce a plate in four minutes; it's the dish that never says no.

The unwritten rule is that fried rice is redemption food: yesterday's rice fries better than today's, its grains dried out enough to sear instead of steam. That thrift logic made cơm chiên the home cook's Monday dish and the street kitchen's margin-saver, and nobody holds it against the plate.

Vietnam also grew its own luxury variants — cơm chiên cua (crab fried rice) done Saigon-style with a mountain of white crab meat, and cơm chiên cá mặn (salted-fish) straight from the Cantonese playbook. In beach cities like Da Nang, crab or seafood fried rice at a quán nhậu, eaten with cold beer at a metal table, is its own small institution.

dishes.name: Cơm bình dân
dishes.desc: Point-and-pick rice eateries with a glass case of pre-cooked dishes; the everyday lunch.
dishes.history:
'Commoner's rice' — the glass-case canteens that fed urban Vietnam through the lean post-war decades and still feed its workforce today. Point at three dishes over rice, pay pocket change, free trà đá. As an institution it may say more about everyday Vietnamese eating than any single dish.

Cơm bình dân — 'commoner's rice' — is less a dish than the operating system of urban Vietnam. The format: a glass case at the street edge, twenty-odd trays of pre-cooked dishes (braised fish, fried eggs, stir-fried greens, tofu in tomato, thịt kho), you point at two or three, they land on rice, you pay roughly the price of a bus ticket. Trà đá arrives unbidden and free.

The genre exploded in the post-war decades, when urban workers needed home-style food without a home kitchen's time, and it remains the country's true median meal — construction crews, office clerks and students eating the same menu at the same steel tables. The related quán cơm phần and cơm văn phòng ('office rice') are just the same idea in office clothes.

For a foreigner it's also the best crash course in the Vietnamese home repertoire: everything in the case is what families actually cook. House rule worth knowing: the free soup (canh) is for pouring over the last of your rice — finishing 'cơm chan canh' is the traditional full stop to the meal.

dishes.name: Xôi
dishes.desc: Steamed glutinous rice, savoury (chicken, mung bean + shallots) or sweet (gấc, coconut).
dishes.history:
Sticky rice is older than the nation's story of itself — glutinous varieties fed the northern highlands and ritual offerings long before breakfast carts. Xôi remains the food of ancestors' altars, school mornings and overnight bus stops: dense, portable calories wrapped in banana leaf.

Xôi is the older sibling of every rice dish in this list. Glutinous rice was cultivated in northern Vietnam's hills millennia ago, and it remains the ritual grain: no death anniversary, wedding or Tết altar is complete without a tray of it, and the [Hùng kings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B9ng_king) origin myth of bánh chưng is a sticky-rice story. Eating xôi for breakfast connects a Hanoi office worker to something genuinely ancient.

The everyday economy of xôi runs on baskets and banana leaf: vendors — the xôi ladies — sell it from bicycles at dawn, wrapped to go, dense enough to hold a laborer until lunch. The savory canon includes xôi xéo (turmeric rice with mung-bean paste shaved over it and a flood of fried shallots, Hanoi's favorite), xôi gà, xôi lạp xưởng; the sweet side runs xôi gấc (dyed scarlet with gac fruit, mandatory at weddings for luck), xôi đậu đen, xôi lá dứa.

Colors matter: gấc red for celebration, and in the northern mountains the Tày and Thái peoples steam xôi ngũ sắc — five-colored sticky rice dyed with forest leaves — for festivals. Xôi is also the polite breakfast for monks and students before exams: solid, auspicious, unfussy.

dishes.name: Cơm lam
dishes.desc: Sticky rice roasted inside a bamboo tube over fire; peel, slice, dip in sesame-peanut salt. Grilled-chicken's partner.
dishes.history:
Cơm lam is cooking older than cookware: sticky rice and spring water sealed in a green bamboo internode and roasted over fire, the bamboo's inner membrane wrapping the rice in a papery skin and a faint grassy sweetness. It belongs to the highland peoples — Thái and Mường in the northwest, Ê Đê and Ba Na in the Central Highlands — for whom it was travel and field food: a sealed, portable, self-cooking meal.

Tourism gave it a second career: peeled and sliced into rounds, dipped in muối vừng (sesame-peanut salt) beside a flame-grilled chicken, it's now the signature meal of Sa Pa, Pleiku and every ecolodge in between. The bamboo matters — too old and it won't perfume, too young and it burns — which keeps the dish stubbornly artisanal.

dishes.name: Cơm nắm
dishes.desc: Rice pressed into dense blocks while hot, sliced and dipped in sesame salt or eaten with pork floss. Travel food, perfected.
dishes.history:
Cơm nắm is the north's answer to the onigiri question: hot rice kneaded and pressed in cloth until it sets into a dense, sliceable loaf that survives a day's journey without refrigeration. With muối vừng (roasted sesame-peanut salt) or ruốc pork floss, it fed soldiers, farmers, students on trains — the whole mobile history of a rice country.

It reads as memory food now — grandmothers pressing rice in cheesecloth for bus trips — but survives commercially at highway rest stops and in the lunchboxes of the nostalgia-minded. The texture is the point: compressed rice chews differently, sweeter and denser, a food you make with your hands and eat with your hands.

dishes.name: Cháo lòng
dishes.desc: Rice porridge with pork organs, blood and crackling; comfort and hangover food.
dishes.history:
Nose-to-tail economics of the village pig slaughter: the porridge pot took everything the butcher couldn't sell by noon. It became Vietnam's great leveller — office workers and drivers on the same low stools — and the canonical morning-after cure.

Cháo lòng is the frankest dish in the Vietnamese canon: rice porridge the color of café sữa (the rice is cooked in the pork-boiling broth, often darkened with blood), crowned with the full inventory of the pig's interior — liver, heart, intestine ruffles, blood pudding cubes, crackling. It descends from the village pig-slaughter, where nothing was allowed to go to waste and the porridge pot was the catch-all.

It's also Vietnam's great social equalizer and its most famous hangover protocol. The gan-and-quẩy crowd at a good cháo lòng shop at 7am includes last night's drinkers, market porters, and retirees who simply like organs done properly. Ginger, rau răm, raw bean sprouts and black pepper are the standard corrective kit.

Foreign visitors tend to arrive via Anthony Bourdain logic — trust the offal dish with the queue — and discover the porridge itself is the point: silky, deeply porky, seasoned by the whole animal. The related cháo huyết (blood porridge with just the cubes) is the budget student version sold outside schools in Saigon.

variants.name: cháo gà
variants.note: chicken porridge with shredded bird, laksa leaf, fried shallots

variants.name: cháo vịt
variants.note: duck porridge, served with ginger fish sauce like bún măng vịt

variants.name: cháo trắng hột vịt muối
variants.note: plain porridge + salted duck egg — the late-night Saigon classic

variants.name: cháo cá
variants.note: fish porridge, gingery, often snakehead

variants.name: cháo đậu xanh
variants.note: with mung bean — the 'cooling' one for sick days

dishes.name: Cháo
dishes.lit: rice porridge — the base of a whole genre
dishes.desc: The rice porridge genre: plain cháo trắng with salted egg, chicken cháo gà, duck, fish, mushroom — comfort at every hour.
dishes.history:
Cháo is where Vietnamese food goes when words like 'dish' feel too formal: rice cooked past surrender into the porridge that feeds infants, invalids, drunks and mourners alike. It's the food of first foods and last foods — the pot that appears at dawn markets, hospital gates and funerals — and the verb 'ăn cháo' can mean simply being taken care of.

The genre splits by intent: plain cháo trắng as a canvas (salted duck egg, fermented tofu, pickled radish), rich meat cháos as full meals, and medicinal cháos — mung bean for heat, ginger for chills — where kitchen and pharmacy blur. Saigon's late-night cháo trắng shops on Hàng Xanh corners are an institution of shift workers and post-club recovery.

Every East Asian cuisine keeps a congee; Vietnam's tell is the topping grammar — fried shallots, laksa leaf, quẩy dough sticks for dunking, black pepper in defiance of blandness. We already cover the offal masterpiece separately (cháo lòng); this entry is the rest of the family.

dishes.name: Bánh tráng cuốn thịt heo
dishes.desc: DIY rolls: boiled two-skin pork, Đại Lộc rice paper, herb pile, green banana/mango, mắm nêm dip.
dishes.history:
Da Nang's DIY table: two-skin boiled pork, a haystack of herbs, and rice paper from Đại Lộc's paper-making villages up the Vu Gia river. Local restaurant dynasties (most famously Trần) industrialised the format, making it the dish visiting Vietnamese most associate with a Da Nang family dinner out.

The dish is an assembly kit, and every component has a hometown. The rice paper comes from Đại Lộc district up the Vu Gia river, where drying racks line the roads; the correct pork is 'hai đầu da' — a cut rolled and boiled so each slice carries two strips of skin bracketing the fat and lean; and the herb tray reads like a field guide: mustard leaf for wrapping, banana blossom, green banana slices, cucumber, và a dozen aromatics you'll learn by trial.

The sauce is the region talking: mắm nêm, fermented anchovy sauce blitzed with pineapple, garlic and chili — sweet, funky, absolutely central Vietnamese. Dip a completed roll and the whole dish snaps into focus; skip the mắm nêm for fear of the smell and locals will judge you gently but permanently.

Restaurant dynasties industrialized the format — the Trần chain made it Da Nang's take-visitors-here dinner, with waiters teaching tourists to roll — but the dish's DNA is the family table: a platter, a stack of paper, everyone building their own. It's the rare specialty that's simultaneously a restaurant genre and a home ritual.

dishes.name: Bánh xèo
dishes.lit: 'sizzling cake' — the sound of batter hitting the pan
dishes.desc: Crisp turmeric rice crepe with shrimp, pork, bean sprouts; central version small and extra-crispy.
dishes.history:
Named for the 'xèo' hiss of batter hitting a hot pan. The south fries them huge and coconut-rich, the centre keeps them small and crisp; its deeper ancestry (Cham? southern folk invention?) is still argued. Now a global street-food star — the crepe that teaches foreigners to eat with herbs and hands.

The name is onomatopoeia — 'xèo' is the hiss when rice batter hits a screaming-hot pan — and the sound is the dish's true birthplace: you hear a bánh xèo shop before you see it. The batter is rice flour, turmeric and often coconut milk; the filling shrimp, pork and bean sprouts; the technique a swirl, a lid, a wait, a fold.

Geography splits the crepe. The southern bánh xèo miền Tây is a dramatic golden wheel the size of a steering wheel, lacy-edged, shared by the table; the central version — what you get in Da Nang and Quảng Nam — is palm-sized, thicker-lipped, crisper, eaten one per person with more attitude. The central one wraps in rice paper with mustard leaf and dips in mắm nêm or a peanut-liver sauce; the southern one wraps in the lettuce itself and dips in nước chấm.

Its ancestry is argued about pleasantly: Cham cooking, southern folk invention, or a cousin of Khmer banh chao — probably all three. What's certain is that it became one of Vietnamese cuisine's best travelers; 'sizzling crepe' night is now a fixture from Paris to Melbourne, though the diaspora version is almost always the big southern wheel.

dishes.name: Bánh khoái
dishes.desc: Huế's thicker, deep-golden bánh xèo cousin with a peanut-liver dipping sauce.
dishes.history:
Huế's 'delight cake' — bánh xèo's thicker, deep-golden cousin fried in small iron pans by the Đông Ba gate for generations. Its liver-peanut dipping sauce (nước lèo) is considered one of Huế's benchmark flavours, a leftover of royal-kitchen saucecraft.

Bánh khoái is Huế insisting that even street food carry the royal city's polish. Smaller and thicker than bánh xèo, fried in a dedicated little iron pan until the shell goes deep gold and stays crunchy to the last bite, it's stuffed with shrimp, pork, egg and bean sprouts and served open-faced, to be cut, wrapped in mustard greens and herbs.

The dish's soul is the sauce, nước lèo: a slow-simmered emulsion of pork liver, fermented soybean, peanuts and sesame — thick, earthy, faintly sweet, unlike any other dipping sauce in the country. Huế people judge a bánh khoái shop entirely on its nước lèo, and the recipes are family property.

The name is a small etymology fight: 'khoái' reads as 'delight', but one story derives it from 'khói' (smoke), for the wood-fired pans fogging the old shops by the Thượng Tứ gate of the [citadel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_City_of_Hu%E1%BA%BF), where the venerable Lạc Thiện shop — run for decades by a family of deaf-mute brothers, a beloved Huế institution — made the dish famous.

dishes.name: Gỏi cuốn
dishes.desc: Fresh summer rolls: shrimp, pork, vermicelli, herbs in soft rice paper; peanut or fish-sauce dip.
dishes.history:
The fresh roll of the southern table, built for heat: no fire, no fat, just assembly. As the 'summer roll' it became one of the world's favourite light foods and a fixture of global 'healthiest dishes' lists — quiet soft power for Vietnamese cuisine.

Gỏi cuốn is southern Vietnam's argument that freshness is a technique. Nothing is cooked at assembly: poached shrimp split lengthwise so they glow pink through the rice paper, sliced boiled pork, vermicelli, lettuce and herbs, everything rolled tight enough to hold and loose enough to bite. The translucent wrapper turns the roll into a display case — good vendors compose the shrimp side deliberately.

The dipping sauce splits into two schools: tương (hoisin-peanut, often with a chili kick) in most of the south, and straight nước chấm for purists; both sides consider the matter settled. The name means 'salad roll', which is exactly right.

Its global career is remarkable: as the 'summer roll' or 'fresh spring roll' it became one of the most recognized Vietnamese foods on earth, a health-food menu staple, and a fixture of 'world's best foods' lists — CNN's readers famously ranked it in their top 50. At home it stays casual afternoon food, sold by the pair from glass carts, the antidote to every fried thing on this list.

dishes.name: Chả giò
dishes.lit: fried meat roll; called nem rán in the North
dishes.desc: Crispy deep-fried spring roll: pork, mushroom, glass noodle in rice paper.
dishes.history:
The festive fried roll of the south (nem rán in the north), obligatory at Tết and death-anniversary feasts. The diaspora made it the world's 'Vietnamese spring roll,' and for many overseas kids it is the single food that means grandmother's house.

Chả giò is the festive fry of the south — the north calls the same roll nem rán — and no Tết table, death-anniversary feast or wedding buffet is complete without a pyramid of them. The filling is a little economy: minced pork, wood-ear mushroom, glass noodles, carrot, sometimes taro or crab, bound with egg and rolled in rice paper that fries into blistered glass.

The rice-paper wrapper is the Vietnamese signature — it shatters rather than flakes, unlike the wheat wrappers of Chinese spring rolls — though modern shops sometimes cheat with wheat wrappers for durability, a scandal in traditional households. Eaten correctly, the hot roll is itself wrapped in cool lettuce with herbs and dunked in nước chấm: fire and garden in one bite.

For the diaspora, chả giò became the ambassador dish — the item every Vietnamese restaurant abroad sells as 'spring rolls', the thing overseas kids associate with their grandmothers' kitchens and New Year. Vietnamese cooks abroad famously repurposed local ingredients decades before fusion was a word.

dishes.name: Nem lụi
dishes.desc: Minced pork grilled on lemongrass skewers, rolled tableside with rice paper and thick dip.
dishes.history:
Huế street food with royal-kitchen manners: pork paste grilled on lemongrass stalks, so the skewer seasons the meat from inside. Rolling it tableside with rice paper and herbs makes it a social dish — eating nem lụi alone is considered slightly tragic.

Nem lụi is Huế's most sociable invention: seasoned pork paste — fatty on purpose, bound with a little pork skin for bounce — molded around stalks of lemongrass and grilled until the stalk's oils perfume the meat from the inside. The skewer isn't a handle so much as an ingredient.

The eating is a workshop: the server delivers a stack of rice paper, a garden of herbs, green banana, star fruit and cucumber, and you slide the meat off the stalk, roll your own, and dip in the same liver-peanut nước lèo that anoints bánh khoái. First-timers roll badly and nobody minds; the table always has a veteran who demonstrates.

The dish radiated out from Huế down the coast — Da Nang absorbed it so thoroughly that nem lụi joints anchor whole streets, and it slots naturally into the city's roll-everything dinner culture alongside bánh tráng cuốn thịt heo. Its cousins are worth mapping: nem nướng in Nha Trang uses the same idea shaped as patties, and both descend from the ancient habit of grilling seasoned pork over coals for feast days.

dishes.name: Nem nướng
dishes.desc: Grilled garlicky pork sausage rolled with rice paper and herbs; Nha Trang famous.
dishes.history:
Grilled pork sausage from the south-central coast; Ninh Hòa near Nha Trang claims the canonical version. Vendor dynasties carried it to Saigon and Đà Lạt, and its garlicky char-and-herb-roll format made it one of the most copied Vietnamese dishes in overseas restaurants.

Nem nướng is the south-central coast's contribution to the grilled-pork-roll universe. The paste is pork pounded fine with garlic, shallot and a little sugar and roasted rice powder, shaped onto skewers or into patties, and grilled to a lacquered blush. Ninh Hòa, a town north of [Nha Trang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nha_Trang), claims the original, and 'nem nướng Ninh Hòa' on a sign is a pledge of orthodoxy.

The Nha Trang service is a production: a platter of the grilled pork with sheets of rice paper, fried rice-paper crackers (the crucial crunch layer), green mango, herbs, and a thick, mysterious dipping sauce built on fermented soybean, pork liver and glutinous rice — every famous shop guards its formula. Bà Năm and Đặng Văn Quyên are the names locals argue over.

The dish traveled with south-central migrants to Saigon and Đà Lạt, and then abroad, where 'nem nuong cuon' became a Vietnamese-American restaurant category of its own (the fame of Brodard in Little Saigon rests on it). Grilled meat plus paper plus herbs is Vietnam's great modular idea; nem nướng may be its most copied module.

dishes.name: Nem chua
dishes.desc: Tart cured raw pork fermented with garlic and chili in banana leaf; a bar snack, not cooked.
dishes.history:
Meat preservation from before refrigeration: raw pork cured sour by fermentation in banana leaf, with Thanh Hóa the most storied producer. What began as necessity is now pure pleasure — the pink, tart, chili-studded bite that anchors beer culture across the country.

Nem chua is charcuterie by tropical logic: raw pork, salt, sugar, garlic, chili and roasted rice powder (thính), wrapped tight in banana leaf and left to ferment for a few days until lactic acid cooks it sour — no fire involved. It's the same preservation chemistry as salami, arrived at independently by people with banana leaves instead of casings.

[Thanh Hóa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thanh_H%C3%B3a_province) province is the sacred origin; its nem chua comes wrapped in a whole architecture of leaves, each parcel with its slice of garlic, sliver of chili and guava leaf inside. Regional descendants abound — nem chua Huế, nem chợ Huyện in Bình Định, and Da Nang's own tré, a funkier cousin spiced with galangal — and every province swears theirs travels best.

Its modern habitat is the beer table: nem chua is Vietnam's default drinking snack, sour-sweet-hot, sliced and eaten with more raw garlic. The deep-fried spin-off nem chua rán became a Hanoi teen obsession in the 2000s, sold in alleys off Hàng Bông. Small warning-shaped fun fact: it is technically raw fermented pork, and Vietnamese eat roughly a national GDP's worth of it anyway.

dishes.name: Bò bía
dishes.desc: Soft rolls of jicama, dried shrimp, Chinese sausage, egg, herbs; sweet-savoury dip.
dishes.history:
Teochew popiah gone Vietnamese: the name itself is a transliteration. Chợ Lớn hawkers shrank it into a two-bite roll of jicama, Chinese sausage and dried shrimp; generations of Saigon schoolchildren have measured pocket money in bò bía rolls.

Bò bía is a Teochew immigrant story you can hold in one hand: the name transliterates 'popiah', the fresh spring roll of Fujian and Chaozhou, and the Saigon version keeps the ancestral filling — jicama and carrot stewed soft, Chinese sausage, dried shrimp, omelet ribbons — shrunk into a two-bite roll and dipped in hoisin-peanut sauce with chili.

Its natural habitat is the school gate and the park cart: bò bía is pocket-money food, sold in pairs, historically by Chinese-Vietnamese vendors whose carts marked the edges of Chợ Lớn's influence. Everyone under fifty in Saigon has a bò bía memory, usually involving exact change and cutting class.

Two footnotes: there's a sweet northern namesake (bò bía ngọt — coconut, sesame, malt sugar in a dry wrapper) that confuses everyone, and the savory original contains no beef despite 'bò' — the name is pure phonetics, a fact vendors have explained to literal-minded customers for a hundred years.

dishes.name: Gỏi cá Nam Ô
dishes.desc: Raw herring cured in lime/vinegar with roasted rice powder, galangal; wrapped with wild herbs.
dishes.history:
From Nam Ô, the Da Nang fishing village that also gave Vietnam one of its most famous fish sauces — raw herring cured in citrus and rolled with foothill herbs gathered from Hải Vân's slopes. A century-old village specialty that survived the village's industrial upheavals and is now protected as local heritage.

Nam Ô is a fishing village pressed between the mountains and the sea at Da Nang's northern edge, historically famous for two things: firecrackers (banned in 1994) and nước mắm Nam Ô, a fish sauce so respected it has protected-heritage status. Gỏi cá is the village's third claim — raw herring (cá trích), the everyday silver fish of the bay, turned into ceremony.

The fish is cured briefly in lime, vinegar or fermented rice, then either served 'ướt' (wet, in a pool of the sauce) or 'khô' (dry, tossed in thính — roasted rice powder — with sesame and peanuts until it looks dusted in gold). The sauce marries the village's own fish sauce with pineapple, chili and crushed peanuts; the greens come off Hải Vân's foothills — wild leaves the vendors can name and you can't.

Eating it is an act of trust in the cold chain of a village that has handled fish for centuries, and the reward is the freshest possible taste of the bay. The tradition survived the village's brushes with resort development — a fight that made national news — and gỏi cá Nam Ô is now served with a side of local pride about having held the line.

dishes.name: Gỏi cá trích
dishes.desc: Phú Quốc's raw herring salad: lime-cured fish, shredded coconut, onion, rolled in rice paper with a peanut dip.
dishes.history:
Phú Quốc island runs on herring the way Nam Ô does — and evolved its own raw ritual: cá trích cured moments in lime, tossed with sliced onion and the island's signature move, fresh shredded coconut, then rolled in rice paper and dunked in a peanut-thick sweet sauce built (naturally) on Phú Quốc fish sauce. Fishermen's breakfast logic: the catch is cleanest before the boat even docks.

It's the southern island cousin of Da Nang's gỏi cá Nam Ô — same trust-the-fisherman premise, different supporting cast: coconut sweetness instead of thính dust, sweet peanut dip instead of fermented punch. Tour groups now eat it by the platter, but the dish predates tourism by generations of boat kitchens.

dishes.name: Bánh bèo
dishes.desc: Tiny steamed rice saucers with dried shrimp, crackling, scallion oil; eaten by the dozen.
dishes.history:
Huế's snacking culture ('ăn hàng') in miniature: dozens of tiny 'water-fern' saucers, each two bites, reflecting the imperial city's taste for smallness and multiplicity over abundance. Afternoon bánh bèo ladies carrying baskets street to street were once the city's clock.

Bánh bèo means 'water-fern cake' — the little steamed rice discs, each with its dimple, are named for the tiny floating fern that carpets Huế's ponds. The dimple is functional: it catches the toppings — brick-orange dried shrimp floss, crackling cubes, scallion oil, a spoon of nước chấm poured on at the last second.

Huế serves them by the dozen in shallow saucers, and the correct tool is not chopsticks but a small bamboo paddle or spoon to lever each cake free in one motion. This miniaturization is pure Huế: the imperial kitchen's aesthetic of many small refined things filtered down into街 snack culture — 'ăn hàng', grazing, as a way of life. Afternoon bánh bèo ladies once walked fixed routes through the city, their call part of the soundscape.

The dish stretches down the coast with dialects: Quảng Nam and Da Nang pour them thicker with a wetter shrimp-pork topping; Quy Nhơn adds peanuts. A tray of bánh bèo chén — dozens of tiny bowls emptied and stacked into towers — is one of central Vietnam's happiest table sights.

dishes.name: Bánh bột lọc
dishes.desc: Translucent chewy tapioca dumplings with shrimp and pork, often in banana leaf.
dishes.history:
The jewel of Huế's dumpling canon: translucent tapioca skin showing whole shrimp inside, steamed in banana leaf. Rooted in royal-adjacent kitchen refinement, it's now a Tết gift economy of its own — Huế families ship boxes of them across the country.

Bánh bột lọc is Huế's glass dumpling: tapioca starch kneaded into a dough that steams translucent, showing off a whole red shrimp and a cube of caramelized pork belly inside like specimens in amber. The 'lá' version is wrapped in banana leaf and steamed into slim packets; the 'trần' (naked) version is boiled and slicked with scallion oil.

The dumpling belongs to Huế's bánh trilogy — bèo, nậm, lọc — usually eaten in one sitting as a progression of textures: soft, silky, chewy. All three come from the same economy of rice-and-tapioca cleverness that let the old capital's cooks produce elegance from starch and shrimp shells' worth of protein.

Modern Huế ships bánh bột lọc nationwide — vacuum-packed bundles ride the night buses to Saigon and Hanoi as edible souvenirs, and Huế families abroad steam them for Tết to teach children what home tastes like. The chew — 'dai' — is the quality Vietnamese prize in it, a texture category Western palates learn to love on exactly this dumpling.

dishes.name: Bánh nậm
dishes.desc: Thin rice batter with shrimp-pork topping steamed flat in banana leaf; Huế trio member.
dishes.history:
The flat, delicate member of Huế's steamed-cake trio (with bánh bèo and bột lọc): rice batter and shrimp-pork pressed thin in banana leaf. Its gentleness is deliberate — food of the imperial city's preference for subtlety, and famously easy on children and the elderly.

Bánh nậm is the gentlest of Huế's steamed cakes: rice batter spread thin as a crepe inside a banana leaf, topped with minced shrimp and pork cooked to an orange floss, folded flat and steamed. Unwrapped, it's a pale rectangle you eat with a spoon straight off the leaf, dressed with a little sweet fish sauce.

Its softness is its cultural role: bánh nậm is what Huế feeds children, convalescents and the elderly — food as bedside manner. It also appears in vegetarian form (mushroom, mung bean) for the first and fifteenth of the lunar month, when much of Huế eats chay in observance of its Buddhist rhythms; the city's vegetarian bánh tradition is a cuisine of its own.

Like its siblings it carries the imperial-kitchen fingerprint — labor-intensive miniatures, banana leaf as both cookware and plate — and it rarely travels alone: order bánh nậm anywhere in Huế and the vendor will assume you want the full trilogy, because you do.

dishes.name: Bánh ít
dishes.desc: Glutinous pyramid in banana leaf; sweet mung-bean/coconut or savoury shrimp-pork.
dishes.history:
The banana-leaf pyramid of ancestor offerings and festival trays across the centre; 'ít' (little) fits its modest, humble-gift character. The black lá gai version from Bình Định is a famous wedding and homecoming gift — sweetness wrapped as etiquette.

Bánh ít is the pyramid-shaped offering of the central coast: glutinous rice dough around a filling, twisted into a banana-leaf tetrahedron that stacks neatly on ancestor altars. The name — 'a little cake' — carries folk etymologies about modesty and an apocryphal princess named Ít; what's certain is that no central Vietnamese death anniversary, wedding gift exchange or Tết tray is complete without a pile.

The fillings split sweet and savory: mung bean or coconut cooked in sugar for the sweet line, shrimp-and-pork for bánh ít mặn, and the naked variant bánh ít trần skips the leaf entirely. The famous regional cousin is [Bình Định](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%ACnh_%C4%90%E1%BB%8Bnh_province)'s bánh ít lá gai — jet-black dough perfumed with ramie leaf, a bride's-family gift so canonical it appears in folk verse about dutiful daughters.

The shape is the point as much as the taste: the leaf pyramid is origami with a deadline, a skill grandmothers test granddaughters on. Unwrapping one neatly — unwinding the leaf ribbon without tearing — is a tiny inherited ceremony.

dishes.name: Bánh căn
dishes.desc: Eggy mini rice cakes from a dimpled clay mould with quail egg, shrimp or squid.
dishes.history:
A Cham legacy from the Phan Rang coast: rice batter baked in dimpled clay moulds over coals. As it spread up the coast it collected quail eggs, shrimp and squid, becoming the sunset street snack of beach towns — cheap, hot, and social by design.

Bánh căn comes from the Cham heartland around [Phan Rang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phan_Rang%E2%80%93Th%C3%A1p_Ch%C3%A0m), where the Cham people — whose towers still stand over the town — bake rice batter in unglazed clay moulds over open coals. No oil, or almost none: unlike the fried bánh khọt it superficially resembles, bánh căn is baked, the clay wicking moisture until each cake is a crisp-edged, custardy little dome.

The batter often includes cooked rice ground in, which gives the lift; the toppings went from plain egg to quail egg, shrimp, and squid as the dish migrated to the coast — Nha Trang made seafood bánh căn a beach-town staple, and Đà Lạt adopted it against the highland cold. The dipping liquids are a genre: diluted fish sauce with xíu mại meatballs, fermented anchovy mắm nêm, or scallion-oil-slicked fish sauce with a raw egg beaten in.

The clay mould itself is the heritage object — a round tray of cups like a muffin tin's ancestor, made in Cham pottery villages such as Bàu Trúc, one of Southeast Asia's oldest surviving pottery traditions. Watching a vendor work twenty cups with two spatulas at dusk is half the meal.

dishes.name: Bánh khọt
dishes.desc: Crispier coconut-rich southern cousin of bánh căn, topped with a shrimp, wrapped in greens.
dishes.history:
Vũng Tàu's crisped, coconut-rich answer to bánh căn, fried in oil rather than baked dry. Beach-town crowds made it famous; a plate of bánh khọt with a mountain of mustard leaves and herbs is now the canonical Vũng Tàu day-trip meal.

Bánh khọt is the extroverted southern cousin of bánh căn: same small round shape, opposite technique — fried in generous oil in a metal pan until the rim turns to lace, the base to crunch, and the center stays custard. Coconut milk in the batter (and often a drizzle on top) plus turmeric give it richness and color; a single shrimp rides each cake, with dried-shrimp floss for the deluxe finish.

[Vũng Tàu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V%C5%A9ng_T%C3%A0u), the beach town at the end of Saigon's weekend highway, owns the dish: shops like the ancient Bánh Khọt Gốc Vú Sữa (named for the star-apple tree it started under) fry thousands of cakes a day for day-trippers, and 'going to Vũng Tàu for bánh khọt' is a fixed phrase in Saigon weekend planning.

The eating format is central-southern standard: wrap the hot cake in mustard leaf and lettuce with green papaya shreds and herbs, dip in nước chấm, repeat past the point of reason. The name is probably onomatopoeic too — 'khọt' for the sound of the spatula knocking the cakes loose.

dishes.name: Bánh cuốn
dishes.desc: Silky steamed rice sheets rolled around minced pork and wood-ear mushroom; northern breakfast.
dishes.history:
Northern breakfast at its most refined: batter steamed over cloth into near-transparent sheets, a craft the Hanoi village of Thanh Trì turned into an identity. The rhythm of the vendor's spatula on the steamer drum is one of old Hanoi's signature morning sounds.

Bánh cuốn is northern rice craft at its most delicate: fermented rice batter ladled onto a cloth stretched over a steaming drum, covered for seconds, then lifted off as a sheet barely thicker than smoke and rolled around minced pork and wood-ear mushrooms. Done well it's translucent, trembling, gone in two bites; the fried-shallot snowfall and nước chấm with a drop of cà cuống (giant water bug essence, for the old-school) finish it.

[Thanh Trì](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thanh_Tr%C3%AC_district), once a village south of Hanoi and now a district of it, is the mother appellation — bánh cuốn Thanh Trì is served unfilled, just the sheets with shallots, the batter's quality standing naked. Hanoi shops steam to order at the doorway, and the vendor's spatula-tap rhythm on the drum is an old quarter sound as familiar as train horns.

The dish maps the country in variants: bánh cuốn trứng in Lạng Sơn steams an egg into the sheet; Hà Nam adds grilled pork on the side; the center and south shade into bánh ướt, the unrolled cousin. Breakfast is its hour — a plate with chả quế (cinnamon pork loaf) and hot broth is one of Hanoi's great cheap mornings.

dishes.name: Bánh ướt
dishes.desc: Plain fresh steamed rice sheets with chả, herbs and fish sauce; unfilled bánh cuốn.
dishes.history:
The unfilled cousin of bánh cuốn, central style — fresh rice sheets eaten warm with chả and fish sauce. It's the plainest possible showcase of rice craft: nothing to hide behind, which is why locals judge shops on bánh ướt before anything else.

Bánh ướt — 'wet cake' — is the minimalist branch of the steamed-rice-sheet family: the same gossamer sheets as bánh cuốn but left unfilled, stacked soft and warm, cut with scissors and dressed. Its plainness is a feature: nothing distracts from the batter, so the dish is a rice-quality audit vendors can't fake.

The central version stakes its own identity. In Huế and Da Nang, bánh ướt pairs with chả (pork loaf), nem chua or grilled pork; the road-food legend is bánh ướt thịt nướng from Kim Long, Huế — sheets rolled around charcoal pork and herbs, dunked in peanut-y sauce. Đà Lạt contributes the curiosity bánh ướt lòng gà: rice sheets with chicken innards, a highland cult favorite.

At its humblest, a plate of bánh ướt with fried shallots and fish sauce is among the cheapest hot meals in Vietnam, and for many older central Vietnamese it's the taste of school mornings — the vendor at the gate, scissors flashing, one plate shared between two kids.

dishes.name: Bánh hỏi
dishes.desc: Delicate mats of ultra-fine rice noodle brushed with scallion oil, served with grilled meats.
dishes.history:
Ultra-fine noodle mats from Bình Định, historically feast food — weddings and death anniversaries — because the fineness signalled labour and respect. Brushed with scallion oil and paired with roast pork, it remains the centre's celebration carbohydrate.

Bánh hỏi is engineering disguised as daintiness: rice vermicelli extruded so fine that strands would collapse alone, woven instead into flat mats that hold together like fabric.
