The mats are steamed, brushed with scallion oil (or in some towns, chive or garlic oil), and used as the starch base for grilled or roasted meats — most famously heo quay, crackling roast pork.

[Bình Định](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%ACnh_%C4%90%E1%BB%8Bnh_province) province is its homeland, and there it's morning food with lòng heo (pork offal) and rice porridge — 'bánh hỏi lòng heo' signs define the road through An Nhơn. Elsewhere it climbed the social ladder: because the fine mats read as labor and refinement, bánh hỏi became feast food, standard at southern weddings and death anniversaries, the platter that signals the family did things properly.

The name likely comes from 'hỏi' (to ask) via a folk story about a curious eater, though linguists shrug. Practical eating note: it dries fast, so good shops steam small batches all day — limp, cold bánh hỏi is the sign to eat elsewhere.

dishes.name: Bánh giò
dishes.desc: Soft steamed rice-flour pudding with pork and mushroom in banana leaf; warm breakfast.
dishes.history:
Bánh giò is Hanoi's warm pocket: a soft pyramid of rice-flour pudding — the flour cooked in bone broth before wrapping, which is the flavor secret — around a heart of minced pork, shallot and wood-ear, steamed inside banana leaf until the whole thing wobbles. Sold warm from baskets and glass boxes on bicycles, it's the definitive Vietnamese 'quà vặt': the small food between meals that structures a northern day.

Its texture has no Western equivalent — softer than dumpling, firmer than porridge, eaten with a spoon directly from the unfolded leaf, often upgraded with a slice of chả, pickled cucumber, or chili sauce. School gates at 4pm and office corridors at 10am are its natural habitats.

The banana leaf matters beyond wrapping: it perfumes the steam, colors the surface faintly green, and makes the bánh giò one of the last everyday Hanoi foods still packaged the pre-plastic way. Watching a vendor unfold one — three moves, no tears — is a tiny masterclass in an economy where the wrapper was always compostable because it was always a leaf.

dishes.name: Bánh bao
dishes.desc: Fluffy steamed bun stuffed with pork, quail egg, Chinese sausage.
dishes.history:
The Cantonese bao, naturalised: Vietnamese versions pack pork, wood-ear, Chinese sausage and a quail egg into one steamed globe. Sold from glowing cabinets on night streets, it's the country's default portable dinner for students and late workers.

Bánh bao is the Cantonese dabao naturalized: the steamed white bun arrived with Chinese migrants and Vietnam customized the interior — seasoned minced pork, wood-ear, glass noodle, a wedge of Chinese sausage and a quail egg or salted-egg quarter, a whole packed lunch in a dome. The Vietnamese version is bigger and busier inside than its char siu ancestor, less sweet, more dinner.

Its ecological niche is portability at odd hours: bánh bao steamers glow at hospital gates, bus stations and night streets, and 'bánh bao here!' megaphone loops from roving motorbike vendors are part of the urban soundtrack. It's what Vietnam eats when it's working late, traveling early, or feeding a kid on the way to school.

The dish also marks the Chinese-Vietnamese layer of every city — the best shops trace to Chợ Lớn families or Hoa communities in Hải Phòng and Da Nang — and it has spawned its own descendants: mini bánh bao chiên (fried), bánh bao xá xíu closer to the ancestor, and convenience-store versions that vendors correctly consider an insult.

dishes.name: Bánh chưng
dishes.desc: The square Tết cake: sticky rice around mung bean and fatty pork, boiled half a day in dong leaf.
dishes.history:
Bánh chưng carries the founding myth of Vietnamese food: Prince Lang Liêu, poorest son of a [Hùng king](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B9ng_king), won the throne not with rare delicacies but with two cakes from the rice his people actually grew — the square bánh chưng for the Earth, the round bánh giầy for Heaven. Every Tết since is a re-enactment.

The making is the meaning: families gather to fold dong leaves, layer rice, bean and pork, and tend the boiling pots for ten or twelve hours — traditionally overnight, the fire-watching itself a vigil that marks the year's turning. A proper bánh chưng is judged on its jade-green rice (from the leaf), its tight square corners, and its balance of bean sweetness against pork fat.

It keeps for days sliced cold, and its best afterlife is bánh chưng rán — leftover squares fried until the rice crisps, eaten with pickled shallots (dưa hành), the canonical Tết-week breakfast. The southern counterpart is the cylindrical bánh tét; the two cakes map the country as neatly as accents do.

variants.name: bánh tét nhân chuối
variants.note: sweet banana center that steams jammy-purple

variants.name: bánh tét lá cẩm
variants.note: Cần Thơ's magenta version, rice dyed with cẩm leaf, coconut-rich

variants.name: bánh tét ngũ sắc
variants.note: five-color showpiece logs for the ancestors' table

dishes.name: Bánh tét
dishes.desc: The southern Tết cylinder: banana-leaf log of sticky rice, sliced into rounds; savory pork or sweet banana hearts.
dishes.history:
Bánh tét is bánh chưng translated for the south: same sticky rice, bean and pork, but rolled in banana leaf into a cylinder and sliced into rounds — the name likely from 'tét', to slice. Scholars read Cham influence into the cylinder shape; southern families read simply Tết, since the log-making marathon fills the days before New Year exactly as the square cake does up north.

The south, being the south, made it richer and more playful: coconut milk in the rice, sweet banana hearts that steam to jam, and Cần Thơ's famous lá cẩm version dyed magenta. Sliced rounds fried crisp with dưa món pickles handle the post-Tết leftovers. A Vietnamese family's cake shape — square or cylinder — remains one of the fastest ways to guess where their grandmother was born.

dishes.name: Bánh giầy
dishes.desc: Chewy pounded sticky-rice discs, usually sandwiching a slab of giò lụa. Bánh chưng's round twin from the founding legend.
dishes.history:
Bánh giầy is the other half of the Lang Liêu legend: the round white cake representing Heaven's dome, presented alongside the square bánh chưng of Earth. Steamed sticky rice pounded in a mortar until it becomes one elastic, snow-white body, it's the same technology as Japanese mochi — the shared ancient grammar of glutinous-rice Asia.

Day to day it has a humbler job: two discs sandwiching a slice of giò lụa, sold from glass boxes as a commuter breakfast. Festival versions get pounded ceremonially — the rhythmic thud of pestles at Hùng king commemorations in Phú Thọ is the dish being made as ritual theater, food and national origin story at once.

dishes.name: Bánh phu thê
dishes.desc: Translucent chewy wedding cake — mung bean and coconut in amber tapioca — boxed in folded leaf like a gift.
dishes.history:
Bánh phu thê is Vietnam's marriage contract in dessert form: a translucent, papaya-tinted tapioca dome showing its mung-bean heart, its chew and sweetness read as the stickiness and sweetness of a good match. Legend traces it to the Lý dynasty court at Đình Bảng (Bắc Ninh), where a king's wife sent cakes to her campaigning husband; the name — and its folk variant 'xu xê' — has meant weddings ever since.

It travels in pairs by design: boxed in folded banana or pandan leaf squares, given in even numbers among betrothal gifts. Huế keeps its own smaller, jewel-like version in clear boxes, another case of the old capital miniaturizing whatever it adopts.

dishes.name: Bánh tro
dishes.desc: Amber, jelly-clear lye-water rice dumpling, cool and barely flavored, dipped in molasses. The Đoan Ngọ festival cake.
dishes.history:
Bánh tro is glutinous rice transformed by ash: the grains soak in lye water filtered through plant ash, then steam into a cool amber jelly with almost no taste of their own — the whole point being the temperature, the slip, and the dark cane molasses (mật mía) it's dipped in. It's the same alkaline technology as cao lầu's noodles and Cantonese kee zhang, one deep root system under many dishes.

Its calendar slot is Tết Đoan Ngọ, the fifth-of-fifth 'parasite-killing festival' at midsummer's turn, when the body is thought to need cooling and cleansing: bánh tro, fermented cơm rượu and sour fruit are that day's prescribed menu. The rest of the year it lingers at markets as the most refreshing thing that looks like amber and tastes like a cold morning.

variants.name: bánh nướng
variants.note: baked, mahogany-glazed; mixed 'thập cẩm' filling or sweet pastes

variants.name: bánh dẻo
variants.note: snow-white mochi-style shell, eaten uncooked; lotus or bean paste

dishes.name: Bánh trung thu
dishes.desc: Mooncakes for the Mid-Autumn Festival: baked bánh nướng and snowy bánh dẻo, salted yolk moons inside.
dishes.history:
Vietnam's mooncake pair — baked bánh nướng and chewy white bánh dẻo — arrives with Tết Trung Thu, the [Mid-Autumn Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mid-Autumn_Festival) that Vietnam turned into the children's night: lion dances, star lanterns, and cakes with salted-yolk moons inside. The classic Vietnamese filling is thập cẩm, a maximalist mix (candied squash, sausage, lime leaf, sesame) that each generation swears is either heritage or punishment.

The cakes run on gift economics: September streets sprout branded stalls overnight, and boxes travel between families, offices and teachers in a circuit as choreographed as the moon itself. Old-line Hanoi bakeries with queues down the block (Bảo Phương on Thụy Khuê, most famously) coexist with hotel couture cakes; the lanterns don't care.

dishes.name: Oản
dishes.desc: Snow-white cones of pressed roasted rice flour and sugar, wrapped in colored cellophane — altar food first, snack second.
dishes.history:
Oản is food built for the gods' side of the table: roasted glutinous rice flour and sugar pressed in a mold into a dense little stupa, wrapped in colored cellophane, and stacked on pagoda altars and family shrines. Its whiteness and dry keeping-quality made it the ideal standing offering — present for weeks, edible eventually.

Everyone who grew up near a northern pagoda knows its second life: lộc — blessed offerings redistributed after ceremonies — meant children got oản, and its chalky-sweet crumble is the taste of temple festivals for generations. Modern versions go neon and towering for big rituals, but the object is ancient: sugar, rice, geometry, respect.

dishes.name: Bánh tráng nướng
dishes.desc: Rice paper grilled over coals with egg, scallion, dried shrimp; the street 'Vietnamese pizza'.
dishes.history:
Invented on Đà Lạt's cold night-market pavements in recent decades: rice paper toasted over coals with egg and scallion until crackly. Backpackers dubbed it 'Vietnamese pizza' and social media did the rest — it's now the emblem of the modern Vietnamese street-snack boom.

The origin story is unusually recent and specific: Đà Lạt night-market vendors in the 2000s began toasting rice paper over coals as a warm snack against the highland chill, smearing it with egg and scallion oil so toppings would stick. The idea spread downhill at internet speed — within a decade every Vietnamese city had carts, and Saigon's Hồ Con Rùa (Turtle Lake) roundabout became a bánh tráng nướng amphitheater at dusk.

The topping economy is anarchic by design: quail egg, dried shrimp, pork floss, sausage, laughing cow cheese, mayo, chili sauce — the grid of squeeze bottles on a good cart looks like a painter's palette. Folded or left flat, it eats crackly-chewy-rich all at once.

Backpackers christened it 'Vietnamese pizza' and the name stuck hard enough that vendors now use it themselves, with irony fully intact. It's the clearest case study of Vietnam's new-generation street food: invented within living memory, spread by students and social media, already canonical.

dishes.name: Bánh tráng trộn
dishes.desc: Shredded rice paper tossed with green mango, dried beef, quail eggs, sate, lime.
dishes.history:
The schoolyard economy of 2000s Saigon in a plastic bag: shredded Tây Ninh rice paper tossed with green mango, quail eggs, dried beef and chili salt. It grew from gate-side vendors into a national teenage obsession and a genre of its own ('trộn' snacks).

Bánh tráng trộn is a supply-chain story that became a teenage religion. [Tây Ninh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C3%A2y_Ninh_province) province, northwest of Saigon, dominates rice-paper production and also invented muối tôm — shrimp-chili salt — as a local seasoning; offcut scraps of rice paper tossed with that salt was vendor thrift. Add green mango, calamansi, dried beef strands, fried shallots, peanuts, quail eggs and rau răm, shake it all in a plastic bag, and the schoolyard economy of 2000s Saigon did the rest.

The dish is engineered for the exact moment of eating: the rice paper starts leathery, softens in the citrus and sauce, and must be finished before it slumps — a ten-minute half-life that makes it inherently street food. Vendors mix each bag to order with gloved-hand speed that borders on performance.

It has spawned a whole 'trộn' genre (bánh tráng cuốn, bánh tráng bơ with avocado-y margarine, DIY kits sold online) and crossed the ocean to Vietnamese diaspora night markets. For anyone who grew up in 2000s–2010s urban Vietnam, the taste is high school itself.

dishes.name: Bánh đập
dishes.lit: smashed cake — you whack it flat with your palm
dishes.desc: Crisp grilled rice cracker fused to a soft wet rice sheet, smacked flat by hand, torn and dipped in mắm nêm. Quảng Nam / Da Nang alley classic.

variants.name: bánh đập hến xào
variants.note: served with stir-fried baby clams — the Cẩm Nam islet classic

variants.name: bánh đập thịt nướng
variants.note: with charcoal-grilled pork alongside, closer to a full meal

dishes.history:
Bánh đập is the rice paper eaten in stereo: a grilled dry cracker and a just-steamed wet sheet pressed together, so every bite is crackle and silk at once. The name is the instruction — 'đập' means to smash — and the ritual is non-negotiable: the vendor (or you) slaps the folded round flat with a palm so the cracker shatters into the soft sheet, then you tear off pieces and drag them through mắm nêm sharpened with pineapple and chili.

Its homeland is the lower Thu Bồn: the Cẩm Nam islet across the footbridge from [Hội An](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%E1%BB%99i_An)'s old town made bánh đập its franchise, served with hến xào — baby clams stir-fried with rau răm — from riverside shacks that also pour chè bắp. The dish rode the fishing-and-rice-paper economy north to Da Nang, where alley shops sell it as the cheapest possible afternoon snack, often literally the price of a parking ticket.

It's also a two-for-one lesson in the region's rice-paper civilization: the same batter, steamed soft today or dried and grilled crisp for the shelf — bánh đập simply refuses to choose. Everything works because of the sauce; Quảng people will tell you a bánh đập shop lives or dies by its mắm nêm, and they are correct. The dish barely exists outside the center, which makes a local shop that does it well a small neighborhood treasure.
dishes.img_query: banh dap hen xao hoi an

variants.name: bánh rán lúc lắc
variants.note: the filling rattles loose inside — 'lúc lắc' means it shakes

variants.name: bánh rán mặn
variants.note: savory Hanoi version: pork and glass noodle, dipping sauce

variants.name: bánh cam
variants.note: southern name; often glazed with a sugar sheen

dishes.name: Bánh rán
dishes.desc: Fried glutinous ball, sesame crust, mung-bean heart — bánh rán up north, bánh cam down south.
dishes.img_query: banh ran banh cam sesame ball
dishes.history:
The sesame-crusted fried rice ball is pan-Asian (jian dui in Chinese), but Vietnam split it by dialect: bánh rán in the north, bánh cam — 'orange cake', for the shape — in the south. The northern ideal rattles: a properly made bánh rán lúc lắc has a filling shrunken loose from the shell, so children shake them like maracas before eating.

Hanoi also runs a savory branch, bánh rán mặn, fat torpedoes of pork and glass noodle sold at school gates with pickled papaya sauce. Fried-dough economics keep the whole family alive: a basket, a wok of oil, and an intersection is a complete business.

variants.name: bánh tiêu kẹp xôi
variants.note: split and stuffed with sticky rice — carb-on-carb, gloriously

dishes.name: Bánh tiêu
dishes.desc: Hollow fried sesame flatbread, puffed like a pita, barely sweet. The 4pm cart snack.
dishes.history:
Bánh tiêu is the Chinese fried sesame bread naturalized into Vietnam's afternoon: a dough round that balloons hollow in hot oil, collecting a sesame crust and a whisper of sugar. Vendors fry to order from carts whose entire signage is the smell.

The hollow is an invitation: the classic move is bánh tiêu kẹp xôi — sticky rice stuffed into the warm pocket — or a scoop of ice cream in the modern cafés that have gentrified it. It shares the fry-oil economy with quẩy and bánh cam; together they're the holy trinity of Vietnamese fried dough.

dishes.name: Chuối chiên
dishes.desc: Flattened banana in crackly rice-flour batter, fried amber. Best eaten burn-your-fingers hot.
dishes.history:
Chuối chiên takes the stubby, honey-dense chuối sứ banana — too small and seedy for export, perfect for frying — flattens it between boards, and fries it in a rice-flour batter that shatters. The flattening is the technique: more surface, more crust, caramelized edges.

It's the 4pm sugar economy of every Vietnamese street, sold alongside khoai lang chiên (sweet potato) and bánh chuối from the same wok. The Mekong runs richer versions with coconut, and modern carts add a drizzle of condensed milk, but the classic is naked, hot, and eaten from the bag while walking.

dishes.name: Quẩy
dishes.desc: Twin fried dough sticks — the mandatory sidekick of phở and cháo, made for dunking.
dishes.history:
The quẩy is the Chinese youtiao with a Vietnamese residence permit, and its full southern name — dầu cháo quẩy — preserves the grim Cantonese legend: the twin sticks represent the traitor Qin Hui and his wife, fried in effigy for a thousand years. Vietnam kept the shape and dropped the grudge.

Its job description is absorption: torn into phở in Hanoi (a pairing the south finds baffling), into cháo everywhere, or eaten straight while impossibly hot. Quẩy baskets balanced on bicycles at 6am are part of the soup economy's supply chain — no serious cháo shop operates without a supplier.

dishes.name: Bánh gối
dishes.desc: Hanoi's fried 'pillow': crimped half-moon stuffed with pork, glass noodle, wood-ear, quail egg.
dishes.history:
Bánh gối is Hanoi's answer to the empanada — a crimped wheat pastry named for the pillow it resembles, stuffed with the chả giò filling set (pork, wood-ear, glass noodle) plus a quail egg, fried to blisters and dunked in the same sweet-sour nước chấm with green papaya.

Its habitat is the winter sidewalk: bánh gối shops cluster near schools and churches (the Lý Quốc Sư strip by the cathedral is famous), woks going from 3pm, plates shared between friends huddled on stools. Summer slows it; the first cold snap of November is bánh gối season's opening bell.

variants.name: bánh đúc lạc
variants.note: northern: cool, firm, studded with peanuts, dipped in fermented soybean tương

variants.name: bánh đúc nóng
variants.note: Hanoi winter: warm soft ladles under minced pork, wood-ear, fried shallots

variants.name: bánh đúc lá dứa
variants.note: southern sweet: pandan-green, coconut milk, roasted sesame

dishes.name: Bánh đúc
dishes.desc: Rice-flour jelly two ways: cool northern slabs with peanuts and tương, or hot bowls under pork and shallots.
dishes.history:
Bánh đúc is what a rice culture makes when it wants tofu's calm without soybeans: rice flour (traditionally set with lime water) cooked into a pale jelly that can be sliced cold or ladled soft and warm. The old northern version — firm slabs with peanuts, dipped in tương — was famine-era filler food that outlived the famine on merit.

Its two modern careers barely acknowledge each other: Hanoi's bánh đúc nóng, a warm bowl under pork and shallot oil, is a beloved winter street genre; the south's bánh đúc lá dứa is a pandan-and-coconut sweet. Same casting technique, opposite destinies — a very Vietnamese fork in the road.

dishes.name: Bánh tôm
dishes.desc: Whole shrimp fried into a sweet-potato-and-flour fritter; West Lake's signature snack since the 1930s.
dishes.history:
Bánh tôm is inseparable from its address: Hồ Tây, Hanoi's West Lake, where vendors began frying the lake's small shrimp — shells on, for crunch — into sweet-potato fritters in the 1930s. The state-era restaurant on Thanh Niên road became an institution: generations of Hanoi couples courted over plates of it, and the dish reads as pre-war nostalgia to the city.

The fritter formula — julienned sweet potato for sweetness and structure, whole shrimp on top like a signature — never needed updating. Eaten scissored into pieces, wrapped in lettuce and herbs, through nước chấm, lakeside if you're doing it properly.

dishes.name: Cơm cháy
dishes.desc: Crispy rice slabs fried golden, topped with pork floss and scallion-chili oil. Ninh Bình's souvenir crunch.
dishes.img_query: com chay ninh binh crispy rice
dishes.history:
Cơm cháy elevates the accident everyone already loved — the scorched crust at the pot's bottom — into a product: rice pressed into slabs, sun-dried, deep-fried into golden rafts, then painted with scallion oil, chili and chà bông pork floss. Ninh Bình, the limestone-karst province south of Hanoi, made it a regional signature alongside its goat meat, and tourist buses leave the province rattling with boxes of it.

The genre now runs from artisanal (fried to order, ladled with rich braising sauce) to industrial snack bags in every convenience store — the rare food that went from pot-scrapings to supermarket shelf without losing its name.

dishes.name: Chạo tôm
dishes.desc: Shrimp paste molded on sugarcane batons and grilled; slide it off, roll it, and chew the sweet cane after.
dishes.history:
Chạo tôm is Huế's most elegant party trick: shrimp pounded to a spring-textured paste, wrapped around lengths of sugarcane, and grilled so the cane steams sweetness into the shrimp from inside — the same inside-out perfume logic as nem lụi's lemongrass, upgraded with dessert built into the skewer.

It carries royal-banquet associations (the imperial kitchen loved food that performed), and survives as a feast and restaurant dish more than a street one: slid off the cane, rolled in rice paper with herbs and bún, dipped, and finished by gnawing the grilled cane like a lollipop. Formal enough for weddings, primal enough to end with chewing on a stick.

dishes.name: Bánh cống
dishes.desc: A deep-fried cup of rice-and-mung-bean batter, pork and whole shrimp, from the Khmer country of Sóc Trăng.
dishes.history:
Bánh cống is fried in the tool that names it: a small metal cup (cống) on a long handle, filled with rice-flour batter, whole mung beans, seasoned pork and a shrimp riding on top, lowered into oil until it releases itself as a golden turret. It comes from Sóc Trăng's Khmer community in the lower Mekong, and the mung-bean interior is its tell — earthier and heftier than bánh khọt's custard.

Eaten the delta way: swaddled in mustard leaf and herbs, dunked in nước chấm. Cần Thơ adopted it as street food and Saigon followed, but shops still advertise 'bánh cống Sóc Trăng' the way soup shops advertise a mother province.

dishes.name: Bánh khúc
dishes.desc: Sticky-rice ball dyed jade with cudweed leaf, mung-bean and peppered pork inside, rolled in whole steamed rice grains.
dishes.history:
Bánh khúc begins with a weed: rau khúc, cudweed, foraged from northern field edges in the cool months, pounded into glutinous dough for its herbal jade color and haylike perfume. Around mung bean and fatty peppered pork, rolled in soaked rice grains and steamed, it becomes the north's most textured breakfast parcel — the outer grains steam into a pearl armor.

Its sound survives alongside its taste: the night cry of Hanoi vendors — 'ai bánh khúc đâââây' — off bicycles with steamer baskets is part of the city's audio heritage, parodied and mourned in equal measure as the bicycles give way to apps. True rau khúc is now scarce enough that much 'bánh khúc' is dyed with other greens; old Hanoians claim they can taste the difference, and they're probably right.

dishes.name: Bánh tẻ
dishes.desc: Slim leaf-wrapped baton of plain-rice dough around pork and wood-ear; the northern village lunch cake.
dishes.history:
Bánh tẻ is the modest sibling in the leaf-wrapped family: made from ordinary tẻ rice rather than glutinous, so it sets firm and clean-biting instead of chewy. Wrapped slim in dong or banana leaf around a seam of pork and wood-ear, it's northern village fare — Sơn Tây and Thanh Hóa both claim benchmark versions, the latter calling it bánh răng bừa, 'harrow-tooth cake', for its shape.

It occupies the practical middle of the bánh spectrum: more substantial than bánh nậm, less ceremonial than bánh chưng — the cake you pack for field work or a bus ride, eaten cool with a slick of fish sauce.

dishes.name: Bánh tai
dishes.lit: ear cake — named for its shape
dishes.desc: Ear-shaped plain-rice dumpling around seasoned pork; Phú Thọ's breakfast heirloom.
dishes.history:
Bánh tai is Phú Thọ province's morning signature — a crescent of plain-rice dough curled like an ear around minced pork, steamed naked (no leaf), pale and cool to the touch. The midlands province northwest of Hanoi treats it as heritage: it's breakfast with a bowl of sweetened soy milk, a pairing locals consider self-evident.

It belongs to the archaic layer of rice-dough cookery — few ingredients, shape as identity — and barely exists outside its province, which makes it a small pilgrimage food: the kind of thing you eat in situ or read about wistfully in food columns about 'the cakes of the ancestors' land' (Phú Thọ being the Hùng kings' homeland).

dishes.name: Bánh quai vạc
dishes.lit: 'cauldron-handle' cake — for the crimped crescent shape
dishes.desc: Chewy translucent crescent dumplings with shrimp, tossed in shallot oil; Phan Thiết's street pride.
dishes.history:
Bánh quai vạc is Phan Thiết's cousin of Huế's bánh bột lọc: tapioca dough rolled thin, folded over whole small shrimp into crimped crescents said to resemble cauldron handles, boiled glassy and tossed with fried shallots. The fishing-port version leans briny — the shrimp are the town's own — and the dipping sauce runs sweet-hot in the south-central style.

Sold by the plate in markets and by evening carts, it's one of those dishes that anchors a town's self-image: ask anyone from Phan Thiết (a town otherwise world-famous for fish sauce and dragon fruit) what to eat at home and this answers first. The fried variant, bánh quai vạc chiên, shades into empanada territory.

dishes.name: Bánh đa nướng
dishes.desc: The sesame rice cracker itself — grilled over coals, the central table's edible spoon and mandatory sidekick.
dishes.img_query: banh da nuong sesame rice cracker
dishes.history:
Bánh đa nướng is infrastructure disguised as a snack: the sesame-studded rice cracker grilled over coals that the central table cannot function without. It scoops mít trộn and hến xào, fuses with wet rice sheets into bánh đập, crumbles over mì Quảng — one cracker, four jobs — and eaten alone with a smear of chili sauce it's a complete afternoon.

Cracker-making villages around Quảng Nam and Đà Nẵng sun-dry the discs by the thousand on bamboo racks (Túy Loan village's are famous, tied to its ancient communal house), and the vendor grilling crackers one at a time over a coal pot, flipping with bare fingers, is a piece of street choreography older than any menu. Not to be confused with Đà Lạt's egg-topped bánh tráng nướng — this is the plain, older, load-bearing original.

dishes.name: Bê thui Cầu Mống
dishes.desc: Flame-seared rare veal sliced thin, wrapped in rice paper with herbs, fermented-anchovy dip.
dishes.history:
From Cầu Mống village by the Thu Bồn river: whole young veal flame-seared over straw so the outside chars while the inside stays rose-pink. A Quảng Nam feast tradition tied to the mắm nêm of the coast — locals consider the sauce, not the meat, the real test.

Cầu Mống is a village by the Thu Bồn river in Quảng Nam, at the old crossing on the Saigon–Da Nang road, and its name has been welded to bê thui — 'seared calf' — for generations of truck drivers and now food pilgrims. The technique is the identity: a whole young calf turned over straw or sugarcane-bagasse fire so the skin chars aromatic while the interior stays rose-rare, then sliced thin to show the gradient.

The plate is a central-coast mandala: the veal fanned out with a stack of rice paper, banana blossom, green banana, herbs from the riverbanks, and the non-negotiable sauce — mắm nêm, fermented anchovy with pineapple and chili. Locals say the sauce is the real test of a shop; the calf just has to be honest.

The strip of bê thui restaurants at Cầu Mống now feeds tour buses as much as truckers, but the dish stays defiantly regional — attempts to sell it in Hanoi or Saigon mostly read as imitations, because the herbs, the mắm and the straw-fire smell are all local supply chains. Eating it roadside, off the highway's shoulder, remains the correct format.

dishes.name: Thịt kho tàu
dishes.desc: Pork belly and eggs braised in coconut water and fish-sauce caramel; the home-cooking icon.
dishes.history:
The southern Tết pot: pork belly and eggs braised in coconut water until mahogany, made in volumes to last the holiday when markets close. The round eggs and square-cut pork carry yin-yang symbolism, and the name's 'tàu' (Chinese? or old southern word for 'braised bland') is a running etymology fight.

Thịt kho tàu is the taste of the southern Tết: in the days before the holiday, when markets close for a week, every household braises a cauldron of pork belly and eggs in coconut water and caramel, because the dish improves with each reheating and can feed drop-in relatives indefinitely. The square-cut pork and round eggs get read as earth-and-sky symbolism; the mahogany color comes from nước màu, sugar cooked to the edge of burnt — the southern kitchen's foundation trick.

The name is a standing etymology fight: 'tàu' usually means Chinese, but southern linguists argue it here means 'braised bland-sweet' in the old dialect of the waterways (as in sông cái Tàu), making the dish natively Mekong. Both stories are told at the same tables, often simultaneously.

The dish's soul is coconut water — the braise liquid of a region planted with coconut to the horizon — and its companion is dưa giá, pickled bean sprouts, whose sourness resets the palate between rich bites. Overseas Vietnamese cook it for Tết in kitchens where coconut water comes in cartons, and it still works its homesickness magic.

dishes.name: Cá kho tộ
dishes.desc: Fish braised dark and sticky in peppery fish-sauce caramel; eaten with plain rice.
dishes.history:
Cá kho tộ is the delta's answer to refrigeration: catfish or snakehead braised hard in fish-sauce caramel until the sauce turns to lacquer, salty-sweet enough to keep for days in the tropics and to demand mountains of rice. The tộ — the squat clay pot — is non-negotiable: clay heats unevenly, chars the sauce at the edges, and that faint bitterness is the signature.

The technique's heart is nước màu (caramel sauce), sugar taken dark over fire, the same trick underlying thịt kho — Southern Vietnamese cooking's secret handshake. Black pepper in quantity, a little pork fat, sometimes coconut water: everything else is family variation, fought over politely at lunch.

The north has its own parallel legend: Vũ Đại village (the setting of a famous 1940s novel by Nam Cao) built a national mail-order industry on cá kho làng Vũ Đại, carp braised twelve hours in wood-fired clay pots, shipped by the thousand every Tết. Two regions, one grammar: fish, caramel, clay, time.

dishes.name: Bò lá lốt
dishes.desc: Minced beef rolled in aromatic betel leaves and grilled; rolled up at the table.
dishes.history:
Beef in wild betel leaf, grilled until the leaf perfumes the meat — a dish with roots in the south's bò 7 món (seven-course beef) feast tradition. Now it's alley barbecue at dusk: smoke, plastic stools, rice paper, and the leaf's incense-like aroma doing the advertising.

Bò lá lốt wraps seasoned beef (usually cut with pork fat for juiciness) in lá lốt — wild betel leaf, no relation to the betel chewed with areca — and grills the parcels until the leaf chars and releases its incense-like aroma. The leaf is the dish: peppery, faintly medicinal, impossible to substitute, growing like a weed along southern fences.

Its formal home is bò 7 món — 'seven courses of beef' — the celebratory Saigon feast format that emerged around mid-century restaurants like Au Pagolac, where bò lá lốt is one obligatory course among seven. Street culture then liberated it: at dusk, charcoal braziers on Saigon and Da Nang sidewalks send up bò lá lốt smoke plumes that function as advertising visible from two blocks.

Eaten street-style, the parcels go over a smear of rice paper with bún, green banana, star fruit and herbs, rolled and dunked in mắm nêm — or northern-style with just nước chấm. The dish is also the friendliest gateway offal-free entry into Vietnam's grill-side dinner culture: no bones, no mystery, just smoke and leaf.

dishes.name: Bò 7 món
dishes.desc: The seven-course beef feast: vinegar-fondue dips, grilled leaf rolls, salads, ending in beef porridge.
dishes.history:
Bò 7 món is Saigon's mid-century idea of celebration: one animal, seven acts. The canon runs from bò nhúng dấm (beef swished through boiling vinegar broth) through grilled courses — lá lốt rolls, mỡ chài caul-fat parcels — to salads and chả đùm, closing with cháo bò, the porridge that ends all Vietnamese feasts. Restaurants like Au Pagolac (from 1930s roots) built the format into a dynasty, and overseas Vietnamese carried it to California, where 'seven courses of beef' became a date-night institution.

It's less a dish than a pacing device — each course is a different technique applied to the same beef, rice paper and herb garden — and eating it is a two-hour argument about which course wins. (The lá lốt rolls also live in this dataset on their own; here they're act four of seven.)

dishes.name: Chả cá Lã Vọng
dishes.desc: Turmeric-dill fish sizzled tableside with heaps of dill; over vermicelli with shrimp paste.
dishes.history:
Hanoi's most famous single-dish restaurant: the Đoàn family began serving turmeric-dill fish around 1871 on what is now Chả Cá street, allegedly feeding anti-colonial conspirators upstairs. Dill in this quantity exists almost nowhere else in Vietnamese cooking — the dish is a preserved fragment of old Hanoi.

Chả cá Lã Vọng carries a street's name, a family's name, and a war story. The Đoàn family began serving turmeric-marinated fish at 14 Hàng Sơn street in Hanoi around 1871; the house doubled as a safe house for anti-French partisans, and the dish fed the plotters. A statue of Lã Vọng — the Chinese sage-fisherman Jiang Ziya, waiting decades for his king — stood in the shop and gave the dish its alias. The street itself was eventually renamed Chả Cá street: the dish ate its own address.

The service is theater with a burner: chunks of hemibagrus catfish (cá lăng, classically) marinated in turmeric and galangal arrive sizzling in a pan at the table, and the diner finishes the cooking, tumbling in scandalous quantities of dill and scallion — dill being nearly absent from the rest of Vietnamese cooking, which makes the dish a botanical anomaly. Underneath: bún, roasted peanuts, and mắm tôm for the orthodox.

The original shop still operates, creaky stairs and all, trading heavily on the legend; Hanoians argue about whether newer specialists (Chả Cá Thăng Long et al.) now cook it better. Either way it's one of the world's great single-dish restaurants concepts — a menu of exactly one thing, perfected across 150 years.

dishes.name: Gà nướng
dishes.desc: Charcoal-grilled chicken with honey or lemongrass-chili; beer-garden staple.
dishes.history:
Charcoal chicken is the backbone of Vietnam's beer-garden and highland cultures alike — lemongrass-chili in the lowlands, honey-lacquered whole birds with chẻo dipping sauce in the northwest. Less a recipe than a social format: chicken, fire, beer, time.

Grilled chicken in Vietnam is a map, not a recipe. The lowland beer-garden version is lemongrass-chili or honey-glazed over coals. The northwest highlands do gà nướng mắc khén, rubbed with the Sichuan-pepper-adjacent mắc khén seed and served with chẻo, a dipping paste of herbs and roasted peanuts. The Central Highlands pair whole flame-birds with cơm lam — sticky rice roasted in bamboo tubes — a format Pleiku and Kon Tum restaurants have turned into a destination meal.

The common denominators are char, fish sauce somewhere in the marinade, and the muối chấm — the dipping salt: salt-pepper-lime at minimum, chili salt or muối é (with the basil-like é leaf) where the kitchen has opinions. The bird is usually gà ta, the muscular free-range 'running chicken' whose chew Vietnamese diners prize over supermarket softness.

Socially, gà nướng is the anchor protein of nhậu — the drinking session — and of the quán lẩu-nướng genre where every table has its own brazier. One bird, one basket of rice paper and herbs, one crate of beer: that's a Vietnamese Friday in most provinces.

dishes.name: Lẩu
dishes.desc: Shared simmering pot: thái (sour-spicy), mắm (fermented), gà lá é (central chicken-basil).
dishes.history:
The communal pot that ends Vietnamese celebrations: everyone cooks, no one plates, hierarchy dissolves in steam. Regional broths (sour Mekong lẩu mắm, Huế's gà lá é, Saigon's Thai-style) map the country; the format itself came via Chinese hotpot and became the national way to mark 'we made it through something.'

Lẩu is how Vietnam ends things: weddings wind down into it, company year-end parties culminate in it, funerals' after-meals resolve into it. The word comes from Cantonese 'lou' (爐, stove) via the hotpot cultures of southern China, but the format — communal pot, everyone cooks, hierarchy dissolves — was adopted so completely that it now reads as simply Vietnamese.

The broth is the regional passport: lẩu mắm in the Mekong (fermented-fish, the deepest end of the pool), lẩu Thái's sour-spicy borrowed from Thailand and naturalized decades ago, lẩu gà lá é from Đà Lạt/Phú Yên (chicken with the peppery é herb, a cult favorite), lẩu cua đồng with field-crab curds, lẩu bò nhúng dấm where beef swishes through vinegar broth. Da Nang's seafood lẩu ends beach days; Hanoi's riverside lẩu boats float on winter evenings.

The etiquette is its own curriculum: greens go in last-second, noodles at the end to drink the broth, the youngest person tends the pot and serves elders first. The rolling boil in the middle of the table is Vietnam's version of a hearth — which is why every celebration finds its way to one.

variants.name: bò kho bánh mì
variants.note: the classic: stew as a dip for a whole baguette

variants.name: hủ tiếu / mì bò kho
variants.note: ladled over rice or egg noodles as a full soup

dishes.name: Bò kho
dishes.desc: Aromatic beef stew — lemongrass, star anise, annatto — mopped up with baguette or over noodles. Café-breakfast staple.
dishes.history:
Bò kho is the French pot-au-feu and daube filtered through a Vietnamese spice drawer: beef shank and tendon braised with lemongrass, star anise, cinnamon and annatto oil until the broth turns brick-orange and the tendon melts. Carrots survived the translation; wine did not — the depth comes from fish sauce and time.

Its social slot is the morning café: in the south, bò kho with a torn baguette is a breakfast as standard as phở, eaten at the same tin tables as cà phê sữa đá. The bread-dipping habit makes it the most Franco-Vietnamese meal still eaten daily, colonial history reduced to a sauce worth mopping.

Every region tilts it — the center adds more chili, some cooks add coconut water, Chinese-Vietnamese shops serve it over egg noodles as mì bò kho — and arguments about whether it must contain tendon are settled the usual way: by ordering another bowl.

variants.name: canh chua cá lóc
variants.note: snakehead fish — the canonical version

variants.name: canh chua cá bông lau
variants.note: river catfish, the delta connoisseur's choice

variants.name: canh chua tôm
variants.note: with shrimp instead of fish

variants.name: lẩu canh chua
variants.note: scaled up to a hotpot for the table

dishes.name: Canh chua
dishes.desc: The Mekong's sweet-sour fish soup: tamarind broth, pineapple, tomato, elephant-ear stem, a blizzard of herbs.
dishes.history:
Canh chua is the Mekong Delta's climate turned into soup: tamarind sourness to cut the heat, pineapple and tomato for sweetness, bạc hà (elephant-ear stem) for crunch that drinks the broth, and whatever the river gave that morning. The finishing handful — rice-paddy herb (ngò om) and sawtooth coriander — is non-negotiable; without it the soup is legally naked.

It's the standard-bearer of the southern family meal: canh chua plus cá kho tộ plus rice is arguably THE southern dinner, the sour soup and the salty-caramel fish designed as a pair. Khmer cooking runs a parallel sour-soup tradition (samlor machu), and the delta's version sits comfortably in that continuum while tasting unmistakably Vietnamese.

Northern canh runs miles milder, which makes canh chua the dish southern transplants miss first — and the one northern cooks accuse of being 'sweet soup with fish in it'. The argument is eternal and both sides keep eating.

dishes.name: Súp măng cua
dishes.desc: Silky cornstarch-thickened crab and asparagus soup with egg ribbons and quail eggs; the wedding-banquet opener.
dishes.history:
Măng tây — 'western bamboo' — is what Vietnamese called asparagus when the French introduced it, and súp măng cua is the colonial velouté gone native: crab, asparagus, egg ribbons and quail eggs in a glossy, cornstarch-silked broth. It became the ceremonial opener — the first course of virtually every southern wedding banquet, đám giỗ and company year-end dinner since the mid-20th century.

Its second life is on the street: evening soup carts ladle it into plastic cups with extra quail eggs and a shot of soy and pepper, selling banquet nostalgia for pocket change to students. A dish that manages to be both the fanciest and the cheapest thing on the block, depending on the tablecloth.

dishes.name: Canh khoai mỡ
dishes.desc: Violet, velvety grated-yam soup with shrimp or pork, rice-paddy herb on top. Southern home cooking's coziest bowl.
dishes.img_query: canh khoai mo purple yam soup
dishes.history:
Khoai mỡ is the purple yam whose flesh, grated raw into simmering broth, dissolves into a naturally thick, lilac-colored velvet — no cream, no starch, just the tuber's own chemistry. Seasoned with shrimp or minced pork and finished with ngò om and black pepper, it's the southern mother's-kitchen soup par excellence.

It rarely appears on restaurant menus precisely because everyone's family makes it, which makes it a quiet test of a cơm bình dân's home-style credibility: a glass case with canh khoai mỡ in it is a kitchen that cooks like somebody's mom.

dishes.name: Ốc nấu chuối đậu
dishes.desc: Northern village stew: pond snails, green banana, fried tofu and perilla in a turmeric broth soured with mẻ.
dishes.history:
This is northern village cooking at its most complete: pond snails for chew, green bananas that go potato-soft and thicken the broth, fried tofu to soak it up, all turned gold with turmeric and soured with mẻ — fermented rice paste, the north's live-culture pantry souring agent. Tía tô (perilla) and lá lốt on top are the aroma signature.

The dish descends from the rice paddy's free protein economy — snails gleaned from the fields, bananas from the garden hedge — and survives today as nostalgia food in Hanoi's 'cơm quê' (village rice) restaurants, where city diners pay comfortably for the taste of a frugality their grandparents didn't choose.

dishes.name: Khổ qua nhồi thịt
dishes.desc: Bitter melon rings stuffed with pork, simmered clear. Bitterness on purpose — and a Tết pun about hardship passing.
dishes.history:
Khổ qua means both 'bitter gourd' and, read literally, 'hardship passes' — so the south eats this soup at Tết as an edible pun: swallow the year's bitterness and let it go. The stuffing (pork, wood-ear, glass noodle) sweetens the gourd from inside as it simmers to jade softness.

Beyond the holiday it's an everyday canh with a fan-club structure: children push it away, adults grow into it, grandparents claim the bitterest batch is the best one. Vietnamese food keeps very few deliberately bitter dishes, which makes this one the cuisine's most philosophical — discomfort, cooked until it becomes comfort.

dishes.name: Bánh mì
dishes.desc: Airy thin-crust baguette with pâté, cold cuts or grilled meat, pickles, chili, coriander.
dishes.history:
The colonial baguette hijacked: 1950s Saigon vendors (the Hòa Mã shop is the usual origin claim) split the loaf and packed it with pâté, cold cuts and pickles to make a one-hand meal. 'Bánh mì' entered the Oxford English Dictionary in 2011 and is now one of the world's most beloved sandwiches — colonial legacy, fully reversed.

The baguette arrived with the French and stayed when they left; the sandwich is entirely Vietnam's. Rationing-era Saigon bakers cut the loaf with rice flour (airier, thinner-crusted, cheaper), and in 1958 the Lê family's shop [Hòa Mã](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%A1nh_m%C3%AC) in District 3 — usually credited as first — began splitting loaves and packing the charcuterie *inside* so office workers could eat on the move. Colonial casse-croûte became one-handed Vietnamese street food.

The interior is a layered treaty between two cuisines: French pâté and mayonnaise; Vietnamese chả lụa, pickled daikon-carrot, cucumber, cilantro, chili; Maggi sauce — the colonial condiment both sides claim. Regional schools argue fillings: Saigon's everything-baguette, Hội An's Phượng and Madam Khánh (canonized by Bourdain as 'a symphony in a sandwich'), Hanoi's more austere pâté line, Da Nang's bánh mì que — the baton-thin chili-pâté stick.

The word entered the [Oxford English Dictionary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary) in 2011, and March 24 is now celebrated in Vietnam as bánh mì day. As a symbol of colonialism digested, repurposed and sold back to the world at a profit, no dish on this list works harder.

dishes.name: Ốc hút
dishes.desc: Small snails stir-fried in lemongrass-chili; sucked straight from the shell with beer.
dishes.history:
Da Nang's snail alleys are a nightly ritual: lemongrass-chili snails 'hút' — sucked straight from the shell with a practiced pop. Shellfish this cheap made ốc culture a working-class social institution: the point is the hour of talking, not the calories.

Ốc hút is Da Nang's dusk ritual: mountains of small sea snails wok-tossed with lemongrass, chili and garlic, served with a rice cracker and a saucer of chili-lime salt, eaten by suction. The name is the technique — 'hút' means to suck — and the local skill is audible: a practiced table sounds like polite popping. Tourists get offered a pin or a lime thorn; locals consider that training wheels.

The snail streets — quán ốc clusters in alleys off Lê Duẩn and around the Cồn market area — run on low stools, shared tables and beer by the crate. A plate costs pocket change, which is the point: ốc culture is about the hour spent, not the calories consumed. The genre includes ốc hút sả (the classic), ốc bươu stuffed with pork, and whatever the boats surrendered that morning.

Da Nang's version is distinctly central: heavier on lemongrass, lighter on the coconut-milk-and-butter excess of Saigon snail cooking. It's also one of the city's most democratic institutions — students, families and shift workers on the same plastic stools, shells piling up as the evening's clock.

dishes.name: Ốc (quán ốc)
dishes.lit: snails / shellfish — the whole stall category
dishes.desc: Shellfish stalls: grilled with scallion oil, tamarind, salt-chili, coconut. Point and graze.
dishes.history:
Saigon's quán ốc scene is one of Asia's great informal food cultures: dozens of shellfish species, each with its canonical preparation, eaten at night over beer. It grew from portside abundance into the city's default third place — office, home, ốc stall.

The quán ốc is Saigon's third place: not home, not work, but the plastic-stool shellfish parlor where the city actually socializes. 'Ốc' on a sign promises far more than snails — clams, cockles, whelks, scallops, crab claws, razor clams — each species with its canonical preparations: nướng mỡ hành (grilled with scallion oil), xào bơ tỏi (butter-garlic, a Vietnamese-French handshake), rang muối ớt (chili-salt), hấp sả (lemongrass steam), cháy tỏi, tamarind, coconut curry.

The economics are studied seriously — whole districts (Vĩnh Khánh street in District 4 most famously) are ốc streets, and food writers have called the quán ốc Saigon's answer to the Spanish tapas bar or the izakaya: small plates, cold beer, loud tables, no hurry. Orders arrive in waves; shells accumulate as scorekeeping.

The culture runs coastal too — Nha Trang, Vũng Tàu and Da Nang all keep their own ốc scenes — but Saigon industrialized it into nightlife. For a visitor it's the fastest possible immersion into how southern Vietnam relaxes: point at three species, trust the sauces, keep the beer coming.

dishes.name: Ốc bươu nhồi thịt
dishes.desc: Apple snails minced with pork and lemongrass, stuffed back into shells, steamed; pulled out by their own lemongrass leaf.
dishes.img_query: oc buou nhoi thit hap sa
dishes.history:
Ốc bươu nhồi thịt is snail cookery with table manners: big apple snails minced with pork, lemongrass and ginger, packed back into their polished shells with a folded lemongrass leaf as a pull-tab, and steamed. Tug the leaf and the whole stuffed morsel slides out in one piece — a built-in utensil that delights everyone the first time.

It's the refined end of the northern snail canon (bún ốc's dressier cousin), served at beer tables and family feasts with a ginger-chili fish sauce. The dish also solves apple-snail economics: an invasive paddy pest turned into the fanciest item on the quán's menu — revenge by steaming.

dishes.name: Gỏi đu đủ
dishes.desc: Green papaya salad with beef jerky, herbs, peanuts, sour-sweet fish-sauce dressing.
dishes.history:
Green papaya salad belongs to a Southeast Asian continuum (Lao tam maak hoong, Thai som tam); the Vietnamese city version leans sweet-sour with beef jerky and peanuts. Sold outside schools and temples for generations — the crunch is the memory of every Saigon childhood.

Green papaya salad is a Southeast Asian dialect continuum — Lao tam maak hoong and Thai [som tam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Som_tam) are the pounded, funky, incendiary end; the Vietnamese gỏi đu đủ sits at the fresher, sweeter end: shredded unripe papaya kept crisp, dressed rather than bruised, balanced toward lime and herbs instead of raw fire.

The classic city version is gỏi đu đủ khô bò — papaya with strips of chewy spiced beef jerky, roasted peanuts, rau răm and a sweet-sour fish-sauce dressing — a snack genre that lived for decades outside Saigon schools and in Hanoi's old quarter parks (where it pairs with dried squid, as gỏi bò khô at the famous Hồ Gươm stalls). The jerky vendors with their scissors, snipping papaya and beef into paper cones, are a childhood memory across generations.

Domestic variants track geography: shrimp-and-pork gỏi in the south, banana-blossom crossovers in the center, and a Cham-influenced fish-jerky version on the coast. It's the lightest possible introduction to the fish-sauce-lime-sugar-chili quadrilateral that underwrites the whole cuisine.

dishes.name: Trứng vịt lộn
dishes.desc: Fertilised duck egg boiled and eaten from the shell with rau răm, salt-pepper-lime.
dishes.history:
Fertilised duck egg, shared heritage with the Philippine balut, born of the delta's vast duck-raising economy. Folk belief frames it as strength food (and bad-luck reversal — 'lộn' means to flip); eaten with rau răm and ginger at dawn stalls, it remains Vietnam's most famous foreigner-test.

Trứng vịt lộn is the shared inheritance of duck country: the Philippines calls it [balut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balut), Vietnam eats it in comparable industrial quantities, and both cultures treat it as strength food. The delta's rice paddies double as duck pasture — flocks glean fallen grain after harvest — which made fertilized duck eggs one of the cheapest complete proteins available for centuries.

The folk-magic layer is the name: 'lộn' means to flip or invert, so eating one is believed to reverse bad luck (giải xui). The correct protocol is precise — eaten in the shell from an egg cup, broth sipped first, then rau răm (whose slight numbing bitterness exists for exactly this pairing), ginger shreds, chili salt. Odd numbers for luck; morning or late-night, never quite lunch.

The northern version (older embryo) and southern (younger, softer) differ enough to argue about, and the street genre extends to trứng cút lộn (quail) by the bagful and vịt lộn xào me — stir-fried in tamarind sauce, a Saigon night-stall favorite. For visitors it's the standard courage test; for locals it's roughly as exotic as a boiled egg.

dishes.name: Tiết canh
dishes.desc: Raw blood pudding set with fish sauce over minced cooked meat, peanuts, herbs. Notorious, traditional, and a genuine health risk.
dishes.history:
Tiết canh is the north's most confrontational tradition: fresh duck (or historically pig) blood kept liquid with diluted fish sauce, poured over minced cooked innards and herbs, and left to set into a crimson jelly finished with peanuts and rau răm. It belongs to the village slaughter economy — the logic that nothing, least of all the blood, is wasted — and to a masculine drinking culture where a shared plate at dawn signals trust and hard-headedness.

Honesty requires the warning, and the decoder gives it plainly: raw pig blood transmits Streptococcus suis, which hospitalizes Vietnamese drinkers every single Tết, and raw duck blood has carried avian influenza; Vietnamese health authorities campaign against the dish annually and many provinces ban its sale. Plenty of northerners now eat it only as memory. It's documented here because you will see it — on menus, at countryside feasts — and a decoder that pretends otherwise isn't doing its job. Admire the frugality; skip the plate.

dishes.name: Dồi
dishes.desc: Vietnamese boudin: intestine packed with blood, fat, peanuts and herbs; boiled (with cháo lòng) or grilled crisp.
dishes.history:
Dồi is the Vietnamese branch of the world's blood-sausage family — cousin to boudin noir and morcilla, invented independently wherever pigs and thrift coexist. The northern classic packs pig intestine with blood, fat, glass noodle, peanuts and a garden of herbs (rau răm, húng), boiled and sliced as the crown jewel of the cháo lòng platter; 'dồi' is cooked through, unlike its raw cousin tiết canh.

The modern star is dồi sụn — cartilage-studded sausage grilled over coals until the skin snaps — which conquered Hanoi's beer streets in the 2010s as skewer food. Grandmother's boiled rounds and the grill-smoke version now coexist: same casing, two centuries of context apart.

variants.name: giò thủ
variants.note: head-cheese cousin: ear and tongue set in aspic, Tết essential

variants.name: chả quế
variants.note: cinnamon-rubbed, roasted till lacquered — bánh cuốn's partner

variants.name: giò bò
variants.note: beef version, peppery, dill-flecked in some regions

dishes.name: Giò lụa
dishes.lit: silk sausage — for its smooth grain
dishes.desc: The silk-smooth pork loaf in banana leaf — sliced into bánh mì, bún thang, xôi, and every Tết tray.
dishes.history:
Giò lụa is Vietnamese charcuterie's white silk: pork pounded — traditionally by two men with wooden pestles in a stone mortar, in rhythm — until the muscle turns to satin, seasoned with top-grade fish sauce, wrapped in banana leaf and boiled into a bouncy, pale cylinder. The pounding (now done by machine, to purists' resignation) is everything: ground pork makes meatloaf; pounded pork makes giò.

It's less a dish than a currency — sliced into bánh mì, ribboned over bún thang, squared onto xôi, stacked on every Tết and wedding tray as the respectable protein. The Hanoi village of Ước Lễ claims the craft's origin and its butchers' diaspora runs giò shops nationwide. A good slice squeaks faintly against the teeth; that squeak is the whole guild's reputation.

dishes.name: Bột chiên
dishes.desc: Rice-flour cubes pan-fried crisp with egg and scallion; soy-vinegar dip.
dishes.history:
Chợ Lớn's after-school classic, cousin of the Teochew fried 'carrot cake' (chai tow kway): rice-flour cubes seared crisp on a flat iron pan, bound with egg. The clang of the vendor's spatula is the dinner bell of Saigon's Chinese quarter.

Bột chiên is Chợ Lớn's after-school masterpiece: cubes of rice-flour cake fried on a flat iron griddle in lard until the outsides shatter, then bound with beaten egg, showered with scallion and pickled green papaya, and eaten with a sweet-dark soy dip sharpened with vinegar and chili. It's the local branch of a Chinese family that includes Singapore's carrot cake (chai tow kway) and Cantonese fried radish cake.

The vendor's spatula is the dish's instrument — the incessant metallic clang-scrape of bột chiên carts is the sound of Saigon's Chinese quarter at 4pm, timed to school dismissal. Every generation of District 5, 6 and 11 kids has a designated cart and a strong opinion about crisp-to-soft ratio.

It survives because it's engineering perfection at a student price point: starch, fat, egg, acid, chili — a complete flavor argument for less than a dollar. Da Lat adopted it against the cold, Da Nang has its pockets, but the dish's soul remains Chợ Lớn's griddles, some of them worked by the same families for fifty years.

dishes.name: Chè
dishes.lit: sweet dessert soup — a whole family
dishes.desc: Sweet beans, jellies, fruit, tapioca, coconut milk; hot or iced, dozens of kinds.
dishes.history:
Chè is not a dish but a universe — beans, jellies, fruits and coconut milk in endless permutations, sold from market stalls where the glass columns of colour advertise themselves. It descends from offering sweets and market culture; every region keeps its own canon, every family its favourite lady.

Chè is not a dessert; it's a dessert *system*. The word covers anything sweet served in a cup or bowl — beans in syrup, jellies, fruit, sticky rice, taro, tapioca, corn, lotus seed — in any combination, hot in the northern winter, over ice everywhere else. Market chè stalls advertise by displaying the components in tall glass columns: a color chart you order from by pointing.

The genre's roots are offering sweets — chè kho and chè đậu appear on ancestor altars and at Tết — plus a Chinese-influenced branch (chè sâm bổ lượng, the herbal 'tonic' cup) and a French-era dairy layer that added coconut milk richness to everything. Every region keeps signatures: Huế alone claims dozens, including the notorious chè bột lọc heo quay — roast pork inside a tapioca pearl, sweet and savory at once, the old capital showing off.

The canonical starter is chè ba màu, the three-color parfait of red beans, mung paste and pandan jelly under coconut cream. From there the rabbit hole includes chè chuối, chè trôi nước, chè thái, and the modern mutations — chè khúc bạch with panna-cotta-like cubes swept urban Vietnam in the 2010s. Chè is also the national teaching example that 'sweet' in Vietnam is an afternoon category, not an after-dinner one.

dishes.name: Bánh lọt
dishes.desc: Pandan-green rice-flour 'worms' in coconut milk and palm-sugar syrup over ice — Vietnam's cendol.
dishes.history:
Bánh lọt is the Vietnamese member of the cendol family that cools the whole Southeast Asian tropics: rice-flour batter pressed through a sieve into boiling water so it 'slips through' (lọt) into short jade noodles, served drowned in coconut milk, palm-sugar syrup and ice. The pandan green and the slippery give are the entire experience.

It headlines chè menus solo or teams up with red beans and grass jelly in chè thập cẩm; the Khmer-influenced southwest also runs a savory cousin (bánh lọt xào) that surprises everyone. On a Saigon afternoon that feels like a sauna, this glass is the argument-ending answer.

dishes.name: Sương sáo
dishes.desc: Black grass jelly — herbal, barely bitter, served in cubes with soy milk, syrup or in every chè that needs cool.
dishes.history:
Sương sáo is jelly made from the mesona plant — 'grass jelly' — a Chinese cooling-food that Vietnam absorbed so completely it grows the herb domestically (Lạng Sơn and Cao Bằng dry tons of it). Boiled, strained and set glossy black, it tastes faintly of herbs and smoke and mostly of cold; folk physiology files it beside nước sâm as 'mát' — heat-clearing.

Its white twin sương sa (agar, from seaweed) shares the stall: together they're the texture backbone of the chè universe, cubed into soy milk, coconut milk, or the mung-bean drink sương sa hột lựu. Street vendors sell trembling black blocks from basins, cut to order — dessert as thermal engineering.

dishes.name: Sâm bổ lượng
dishes.desc: The Chinese-Vietnamese tonic cup: lotus seeds, longan, seaweed, jujube, barley over ice — dessert as medicine.
dishes.history:
Sâm bổ lượng is Chợ Lớn's edible pharmacy: the Cantonese tonic soup ching bo leung — lotus seed, dried longan, red jujube, pearl barley, seaweed strands, sometimes ginkgo — iced into a southern dessert. Every ingredient carries a job in the Chinese dietary ledger (lotus calms, longan builds blood, barley drains damp); the drinker mostly notices it's sweet, various and profoundly cooling.

It anchors the Chinese-Vietnamese chè stalls of District 5 and the old herbal cafés where it shares menu space with nước sâm jugs (its liquid cousin, already in this dataset). One cup is a small tour of a medicine cabinet that decided to taste good.

dishes.name: Chè bắp
dishes.desc: Sweet milky corn pudding; specialty of Cẩm Nam islet in Hội An.
dishes.history:
Hội An's Cẩm Nam islet grows sweet young corn on river silt, and the islet's chè bắp — corn simmered milky with glutinous rice — became famous enough that 'going to Cẩm Nam for chè' is a local idiom. Seasonal, humble, and fiercely place-bound.

Cẩm Nam is an islet in the Thu Bồn river a footbridge away from Hội An's old town, and its silt grows famously sweet young corn. Chè bắp — corn simmered with glutinous rice into a milky, barely-sweet pudding, finished with coconut cream — is the islet's franchise, historically sold from clusters of riverside shacks that also serve the local trinity of bánh đập (smashed rice crackers with mussels) and hến trộn.

The dish is seasonal in spirit: best when the corn harvest peaks, cut fresh daily, the cobs simmered in the pot for extra sweetness. It's sweet the way corn is sweet, not the way sugar is — closer to a breakfast porridge than a dessert, and often eaten warm even in the heat.

'Qua Cẩm Nam ăn chè bắp' — crossing to Cẩm Nam for corn pudding — is a fixed phrase in Hội An life, a cheap afternoon ritual predating the tourism era. The islet has gentrified with cafes since, but the corn pudding shacks persist, multi-generation family operations with the river view included in the price.

dishes.name: Chè trôi nước
dishes.desc: Glutinous rice balls with mung-bean centres in warm ginger syrup, coconut cream.
dishes.history:
Floating rice balls in ginger syrup, tied to the northern Hàn Thực festival (bánh trôi) and to reunion symbolism — round things floating together. The southern version grew larger and richer with coconut cream; grandmothers deploy it as edible affection.

The floating rice ball is one of East Asia's oldest shared desserts — China's tangyuan crossed south long ago — and Vietnam keeps two dialects. The north eats bánh trôi and bánh chay on Tết Hàn Thực (the Cold Food festival, third lunar month), when families shape the balls together and the poet Hồ Xuân Hương's famous 18th-century verse comparing a woman's fate to the bobbing bánh trôi gets re-quoted; the south eats chè trôi nước year-round, bigger balls with mung-bean hearts in a ginger syrup deepened with pandan, under coconut cream and sesame.

The symbolism does heavy lifting: round things floating together mean reunion and completeness, so the dessert appears at weddings, full-month celebrations for newborns, and the winter solstice in Chinese-Vietnamese households. The chewy glutinous shell — 'dẻo' — is itself the auspicious texture of togetherness.

Practical romance aside, it's also simply the best cold-evening dessert in the repertoire: served warm, the ginger syrup doing the work of a scarf. Street versions sell from the same carts as chè chuối and tàu hũ, ladled from cauldrons that steam theatrically after dark.

variants.name: bánh trôi / bánh chay
variants.note: the northern Hàn Thực cousins — small plain floating balls (trôi) and stuffed ones in thickened syrup (chay)

dishes.name: Chè xoa xoa
dishes.desc: Iced central dessert: seaweed jelly, longan, beans, syrup. Da Nang summer cup.
dishes.img_query: che xoa xoa hat luu da nang
dishes.history:
Da Nang's summer glass: agar jelly ('xoa xoa') from the coast's seaweed harvest, with beans, longan and syrup over ice. It rode the city's beach culture from harbour-side carts to every market — the local answer to the question 'how do you survive April here?'

Xoa xoa is the central coast's name for agar jelly — set from seaweed harvested off the very beaches Da Nang now advertises — and chè xoa xoa hạt lựu is the city's definitive summer glass: cubes of the clean-tasting jelly with 'pomegranate seeds' (hạt lựu — water chestnut kernels jacketed in red tapioca), black-eyed or red beans, coconut milk and shaved ice.

The dish's home turf is the market: Chợ Cồn's chè alley has served office workers and schoolkids the same glass for generations, and the vendors' assembly line — jelly, beans, ice, coconut, done — is one of the market's small spectacles. Price and speed are the point; it's a ten-minute institution.

Culinarily it's the lightest member of the chè family — barely sweet, mostly texture and cold — which is exactly what 38-degree April in Da Nang requires. The seaweed-jelly tradition also connects it to a wider central-coast habit of eating the sea's vegetables: rau câu jellies, seaweed soups, the coast feeding itself twice over.

dishes.name: Yến sào
dishes.desc: Edible swiftlet nests — solidified saliva — simmered into a tonic sweet soup with rock sugar. Vietnam's most expensive spoonful.
dishes.history:
Yến sào is the strangest luxury in the Vietnamese pantry: nests that cave swiftlets build from their own hardened saliva, harvested for centuries from the sea cliffs of Khánh Hòa (Nha Trang's offshore islands were an imperial monopoly under the Nguyễn kings, guarded like treasure). Double-boiled with rock sugar into chè yến, the nest turns to delicate glass noodles of albumen — the taste is almost nothing; the point is the tonic mythology: skin, lungs, longevity, recovery.

Modern Vietnam industrialized it — purpose-built 'swiftlet hotels' broadcasting bird calls now line southern coasts, and bottled 'nước yến' sells in every mini-mart, homeopathically dosed. The real thing remains gift-economy gold: a red-boxed set of nests says respect for elders louder than any liquor. Worth decoding mostly so the menu line 'chè tổ yến' doesn't surprise you at ten times the price of every other dessert.

dishes.name: Tàu hũ / Tào phớ
dishes.desc: Warm silken tofu in ginger sugar syrup, ladled from a clay urn.
dishes.history:
Chinese douhua carried by shoulder-pole vendors into every Vietnamese town: warm silken tofu ladled thin from a clay urn into ginger syrup. The vendor's call ('tàu hũũũ...') is a nostalgia trigger for generations; north says tào phớ, south says tàu hũ, everyone means childhood.

Douhua — silken tofu pudding — is one of the Chinese diaspora's great gifts to Southeast Asian streets, and Vietnam split it in two. The north's tào phớ is ladled in thin sheets from an aluminum urn into light jasmine-scented sugar water; the south's tàu hũ nước đường runs warmer and deeper — ginger syrup, sometimes coconut milk, pandan, tapioca pearls. Both descend from the shoulder-pole vendors whose call ('phớớớ…' in Hanoi, 'tàu hũũũ…' in Saigon) is a documented piece of urban folklore, nostalgic enough to appear in memoirs and pop songs.

The serving gesture is the craft: the flat spoon skims the just-set curd in translucent leaves without breaking it — a skill vendors demonstrate with studied nonchalance. Warm in winter, over ice in the south's eternal summer, it's protein masquerading as dessert, historically the cheapest sweet thing a child could buy.

The modern era added toppings (grass jelly, taro balls, the Taiwanese douhua influence circling back), but the classic remains a bowl, a ladle of syrup, a scatter of ginger heat. For older Vietnamese the sound of the vendor's call is the dish; the taste is almost secondary.

dishes.name: Sữa chua nếp cẩm
dishes.desc: Tangy yogurt over fermented purple-black glutinous rice; café favourite.
dishes.history:
A collision of two legacies: French-introduced yogurt culture and the northern highlands' black sticky rice, fermented sweet. Cafés in Hanoi and Hải Phòng made the pairing a craze that went national — tangy over winey, cold over warm.

This parfait is two histories in one cup. Yogurt (sữa chua, or da ua from French 'yaourt') is a colonial-dairy legacy that Vietnam made its own — homemade in recycled glass jars, sweetened with condensed milk, a fridge staple in every guesthouse. Nếp cẩm is the north's 'forbidden'-style black glutinous rice, fermented a few days with yeast into cơm rượu nếp cẩm: winey, faintly alcoholic, traditionally eaten on Đoan Ngọ festival morning to 'kill the parasites' (giết sâu bọ), as the folk custom charmingly puts it.

Some Hanoi or Hải Phòng cafe in the 2000s (both cities claim it) stacked the two — cold tangy yogurt over warm boozy purple rice — and the combination went national within a decade: chains, night markets, home kits. It's now as standard as cà phê sữa đá on northern cafe menus.

The genius is the collision itself: dairy-sour against rice-sweet, cold against room-warm, French-era technique against a festival food old enough to predate the French by centuries. It's Vietnam's whole culinary method — absorb, combine, improve — in a single glass with a long spoon.

dishes.name: Cơm rượu
dishes.desc: Sticky rice fermented days into winey, faintly fizzy sweetness — northern loose grains or southern rolled balls in their own nectar.
dishes.history:
Cơm rượu is rice caught midway to wine: steamed glutinous rice inoculated with yeast balls (men) and left a few days until the grains turn sweet, winey and barely alcoholic, swimming in their own nectar. The north eats it as loose grains (rượu nếp, often the purple-black nếp cẩm), the south rolls it into little balls in syrup, and the center splits the difference.

Its calendar peak is Tết Đoan Ngọ — the midsummer 'parasite-killing' morning when tradition prescribes eating fermented rice on an empty stomach to scour the system, a folk deworming everyone enjoys regardless of efficacy. Off-festival it tops sữa chua nếp cẩm (already in this dataset) and flavors a generation of café drinks. Grandmothers judge each other's batches by fizz.

dishes.name: Kem bơ
dishes.desc: Blended frozen avocado with coconut ice cream and coconut shreds; Da Nang signature treat.
dishes.history:
Da Nang's Bắc Mỹ An market invention: Đắk Lắk avocados blended thick and frozen, capped with coconut ice cream. A single market stall's idea became the city's dessert mascot in the 2010s, with queues of domestic tourists treating it as a pilgrimage stop.

Kem bơ is Da Nang's dessert flex: 'bơ' means both butter and avocado in Vietnamese, and the dish is the fruit at its most buttery — Đắk Lắk highland avocados blended dense and cold, topped with a scoop of coconut ice cream, coconut shreds and sometimes a drizzle of condensed milk. Not a smoothie (too thick), not quite ice cream: a spoon-standing hybrid.

Its origin is unusually traceable: stalls around Bắc Mỹ An market — Cô Vân most famously — made it a neighborhood habit decades ago, and the 2010s domestic-tourism boom turned the market lane into a pilgrimage site with queues on summer evenings. Đà Lạt runs a parallel claim with its own kem bơ tradition (Thanh Thảo's shop being the landmark), and the two cities politely ignore each other's version.

The dish rides Vietnam's avocado calendar — the highland harvest runs roughly March to September — and vendors will tell you honestly when the fruit is off-season and the blend is thinner. At its peak it's the best two dollars in Da Nang: cold, fatty, barely sweet, engineered for the exact climate outside the market door.

dishes.name: Bánh flan
dishes.desc: Vietnamese crème caramel, often over crushed ice with coffee poured on top.
dishes.history:
French crème caramel, kept after the French left and made Vietnamese: steamed in bulk, served over crushed ice, often drowned in coffee. It survived because it fit — condensed milk, eggs and a café culture that likes its sweets cold and cheap.

Flan is the French crème caramel that refused repatriation. Colonial kitchens taught the technique; Vietnamese kitchens adapted it to the pantry that existed — condensed milk instead of scarce fresh cream, steaming instead of ovens (which most Vietnamese kitchens never had), and portioning into small tins sold cold from street carts and cafes.

The southern serving style is the giveaway that it's been fully naturalized: bánh flan arrives over crushed ice with a shot of black coffee or coffee syrup poured on top — 'flan cà phê đá' — plus sometimes coconut milk, turning a plated French dessert into street refreshment. Huế and Hanoi keep tidier versions; Saigon piles it into plastic cups by the half-dozen for takeaway.

It shares the caramel gene with the savory canon (the same burnt-sugar logic as nước màu in thịt kho), which may be why it never felt foreign for long. Alongside bánh mì and cà phê sữa, flan completes the trio of colonial imports Vietnam kept, improved, and priced for schoolchildren.

dishes.name: Bánh da lợn
dishes.lit: pig-skin cake — for its layered chew
dishes.desc: Striped steamed jelly-cake: pandan-green tapioca layers alternating with gold mung bean, peeled apart layer by layer.
dishes.history:
Bánh da lợn earns its odd name — 'pig skin cake' — from texture alone: steamed layers of pandan-scented tapioca and mung-bean paste with exactly the springy, slightly rubbery bite of the reference material. No pork is involved; children have been reassured of this for generations, usually mid-bite while peeling the layers apart, which is the universal (and correct) way to eat it.

It belongs to the southern steamed-sweet canon alongside bánh chuối hấp — coconut milk, pandan, market trays cut to order — and its green-gold stripes make it the most photogenic thing on the bánh ngọt table. Durian versions exist for the committed.

variants.name: bánh chuối hấp
variants.note: steamed: translucent chewy slabs of banana and tapioca under coconut sauce

variants.name: bánh chuối nướng
variants.note: baked: bread-pudding style with soaked baguette, caramelized edges

dishes.name: Bánh chuối
dishes.desc: The banana sweet, two ways: chewy steamed slabs or baguette-based baked pudding, both under salty-sweet coconut sauce.
dishes.history:
Bánh chuối is what the south does with the daily surplus of chuối sứ bananas: steam them into translucent, ruby-tinged tapioca slabs (hấp) or bake them with day-old baguette and coconut milk into a caramel-edged pudding (nướng) — the second being one of the great colonial-leftover recipes, French bread meeting Mekong bananas. Both arrive under coconut sauce with a saline pinch and roasted peanuts.

The baked version's perfume — banana caramelizing against coconut — sells itself from a block away, and market ladies cut it in generous trapezoids that never survive the ride home. Comfort baking, tropics edition.

dishes.name: Bánh cốm
dishes.desc: Emerald cake of young autumn rice (cốm) around mung-bean sweetness — Hanoi's betrothal-gift classic.
dishes.history:
Bánh cốm begins with cốm itself — immature glutinous rice, harvested milky and pounded flat in the villages around Hanoi (Vòng village above all), the taste of the northern autumn. Kneaded with sugar into an emerald skin around mung-bean paste, it becomes the city's ceremonial cake: no traditional Hanoi betrothal (lễ ăn hỏi) is complete without stacked boxes of bánh cốm from Hàng Than street, where houses like Nguyên Ninh have pressed them since the 19th century.

The flavor is delicate to the point of philosophy — grassy young rice, quiet sweetness — and its short shelf life is part of the gift's meaning: freshness, season, intention. Autumn in Hanoi tastes like this or it doesn't count.

dishes.name: Bánh đậu xanh
dishes.desc: Hải Dương's powdery mung-bean shortcake — gold cubes that dissolve on the tongue, built for green tea.
dishes.history:
Bánh đậu xanh is engineered to vanish: roasted mung-bean flour, sugar and oil pressed into little gold ingots that hold their shape until the tongue, where they dissolve into sweet dust. Hải Dương province owns the craft — legend says a 1930s emperor's praise made the town's fortune — and its highway position between Hanoi and Hạ Long made the yellow boxes the default road-trip and ancestor-altar gift of the north.

The cake demands tea: bitter green trà mạn against the melting sweetness is the canonical pairing, a small northern ceremony of oppositions. Brands like Bảo Hiên rồng vàng trade on century-old goodwill; the recipe hasn't needed to move.

dishes.name: Bánh pía
dishes.desc: Sóc Trăng's Teochew flaky pastry: a thousand skins around durian-mung paste and salted yolk. The durian gift box.
dishes.history:
Bánh pía came south with Teochew migrants (the name is Chaozhou dialect for pastry) and found its destiny in Sóc Trăng, where Mekong durian met the ancestral flaky-skin technique. The modern classic wraps whisper-thin pastry layers around durian-perfumed mung paste and a salted duck yolk — sweet, funky and saline in one bite, with the durian announcing itself through the box, the bag and sometimes the bus.

Vũ Hưng and Tân Huê Viên factories turned the town into a pastry pilgrimage stop on the delta highway, and stacks of the flat gold cakes are the standard 'I went west' gift. Mooncake's tropical cousin, available year-round and unapologetic about the smell.

dishes.name: Bánh gai
dishes.desc: Ink-black glutinous cake perfumed with ramie leaf, mung-coconut heart, wrapped in dried banana leaf.
dishes.history:
Bánh gai gets its lacquer-black color and haunting herbal depth from lá gai — ramie leaf, boiled and pounded into the glutinous dough — around a heart of mung bean, coconut and candied fat, wrapped in rustling dried banana leaf.
