Northern towns compete on pedigree: Nam Định's Bà Thi cakes and Thanh Hóa's Tứ Trụ village both claim the benchmark, sold in leaf-tied bundles at every train stop between them.

It's the northern sibling of the central coast's bánh ít lá gai (see bánh ít): same leaf, same logic, different shape and ceremony. Ancestor altars, death anniversaries and long train rides — the black cake serves all three with equal dignity.

dishes.name: Bánh in
dishes.lit: printed cake — pressed in carved molds
dishes.desc: Huế's moon-white molded cakes, stamped with longevity characters, wrapped in five-color glass paper for the altar and tea table.
dishes.history:
Bánh in is Huế's typography in sugar: roasted glutinous or mung-bean flour pressed in carved wooden molds that 'print' (in) characters — longevity, blessing — onto moon-white tablets, wrapped in translucent five-color paper. The court ordered them by the thousand for Tết and royal anniversaries; the craft village of Kim Long still supplies pagodas and altars citywide.

They're drier and quieter than the mung-bean cakes of the north — built to sit on an altar through weeks of incense and still crumble properly into jasmine tea afterward. Eating one is participating in the afterlife of an offering, which in Huế is most of what dessert means.

dishes.name: Bánh khảo
dishes.desc: Highland Tết cake of the Tày-Nùng: roasted sticky-rice flour pressed around peanut-sesame sugar, papery and dry-sweet.
dishes.history:
Bánh khảo is the Tết signature of the Tày and Nùng peoples of Vietnam's northeastern mountains: sticky rice roasted, milled fine, rested against dew to soften, then pressed in wooden frames around a seam of ground peanut, sesame and sugar, and wrapped in squares of colored paper. Dry, crumbly and gently sweet, it keeps for the whole first lunar month — engineering for a festival that outlasts fresh food.

In Cao Bằng and Lạng Sơn no ancestral altar goes without the paper-wrapped stacks, and the cake doubles as the border region's tea companion. It's a cousin of bánh in and bánh đậu xanh in the pressed-flour family — same idea, higher altitude, older paper.

dishes.name: Bánh mật
dishes.desc: Glutinous dough kneaded with dark cane molasses, mung heart, steamed in banana leaf — the amber village sweet.
dishes.history:
Bánh mật is the taste of northern villages before white sugar: glutinous rice dough kneaded with mật mía — dark cane molasses — into a deep amber, steamed in banana leaf around mung bean, chewy and warm with almost-burnt sweetness. It shares the molasses pantry with bánh gai and bánh tro's dipping cup; together they mark the old sugar economy of the northern midlands, where cane presses ran between harvests.

Nghệ An and Thanh Hóa keep it as market and festival fare. It's rarely glamorous and never exported — which is exactly the register of sweet the countryside trusts most.

dishes.name: Bánh nhãn
dishes.lit: longan cake — named for the shape, no longan inside
dishes.desc: Marble-sized fried glutinous-egg balls glazed in sugar — crunchy, longan-shaped, dangerously poppable.
dishes.history:
Bánh nhãn contains no longan — the little fried spheres of glutinous flour and egg, glazed in cooked sugar, simply match the fruit's size and gloss. Hải Hậu district in Nam Định built the specialty on its famously good glutinous rice, and tins of the pale-gold marbles travel the country as the province's calling card alongside bánh gai.

The engineering is in the crunch: fried slowly so they hollow slightly and crisp all the way through, then tumbled in syrup that dries to a thin frost. Somewhere between a doughnut, a candy and a game — nobody has ever eaten just one.

dishes.name: Bánh cáy
dishes.lit: 'crab-roe cake' — for its orange gấc flecks
dishes.desc: Thái Bình's festival loaf: puffed sticky rice, gấc-orange candied flecks, peanut, sesame and ginger pressed into sliceable bars.
dishes.history:
Bánh cáy is Thái Bình province's edible mosaic: glutinous rice puffed and candied, tinted orange with gấc fruit (the flecks resemble cáy crab roe, hence the name), bound with malt syrup, peanuts, sesame, candied pork fat and warming ginger, pressed into logs and sliced. Nguyên Xá village claims the original and the story that it was once offered to kings.

It's a Tết cake built like provisions — dense, keeping, sliceable for guests all month — and its ginger-warmth against the northern New Year cold is deliberate. With tea, one slice explains the entire northern-delta festival pantry: rice, malt, peanut, patience.

dishes.name: Bánh kẹp lá dứa
dishes.desc: Pandan-green coconut waffles off a charcoal iron — crisp edges, custardy middle, the smell that sells itself.
dishes.img_query: banh kep la dua pandan waffle
dishes.history:
The waffle iron arrived with the French; the south filled it with coconut milk, rice flour and pandan juice and never looked back. Bánh kẹp lá dứa — 'pressed pandan cake' — is the green-gold street waffle whose perfume (pandan caramelizing over charcoal) does the advertising for carts outside schools and markets across the Mekong and Saigon.

It belongs to the same Franco-tropical family as bánh flan: colonial technique, canned-and-garden ingredients, street price point. The best vendors run irons blackened by decades and argue about crisp-to-chew ratio the way Hanoi argues about phở.

dishes.name: Bánh rế
dishes.lit: 'trivet cake' — a fried lattice like a wire stand
dishes.desc: Shredded sweet potato fried into a lacy bird's-nest disc, lacquered with caramel — Phan Thiết's crunch souvenir.
dishes.history:
Bánh rế shreds sweet potato (or cassava) into threads, swirls them in hot oil into a lattice disc — named for the rế, the woven trivet under village cooking pots — and drizzles the fried nest with molten cane sugar that sets to lacquer. The result shatters like caramel glass and eats like the platonic county-fair snack, invented decades before anyone needed the comparison.

Phan Thiết and neighboring Phan Rang claim it as their market signature, stacked in amber towers that survive bus rides better than any soft cake. One of those sweets whose entire recipe is visible at a glance and still hard to make well.

dishes.name: Bánh tai heo
dishes.desc: Crunchy fried spiral biscuits striped like a pig's ear — the tin-jar childhood snack of every Vietnamese shop shelf.
dishes.img_query: banh tai heo pig ear biscuit
dishes.history:
Bánh tai heo is the pig's ear rendered in pastry: two doughs — one plain, one cocoa- or coffee-tinted — rolled together, sliced into striped spirals and fried hard. The French palmier is its distant cousin, but the Vietnamese version is thinner, crunchier and lives not in patisseries but in great glass jars at tạp hóa corner shops, sold by the scoop into paper bags.

It's pure childhood infrastructure: the snack bought with the smallest coin, the jar at grandmother's house, the thing that turns up at Tết when fancier boxes run out. Industrial bags now dominate, but the hand-fried market version — irregular, extra-scorched at the edges — remains the connoisseur's spiral.

dishes.name: Mè xửng
dishes.desc: Huế's amber sesame chew: translucent malt-peanut taffy sheeted in sesame, cut in squares, sold in every tourist bag.
dishes.history:
Mè xửng is Huế's candy signature: malt syrup, sugar, peanuts and tapioca cooked to a translucent amber chew, rolled in toasted sesame and cut into stamp-sized squares that stretch languidly when pulled apart. The craft predates the tourist trade — court-era Huế already prized the candy with tea — but the trade perfected the box: no visitor leaves the city without one, usually pressed on them twice.

Brands like Thiên Hương keep factory-scale tradition, while old shops argue the proper chew (softer 'dẻo' versus brittle 'giòn'). With bitter tea on a rainy Huế afternoon — the city's default weather and mood — the candy makes complete sense.

dishes.name: Kẹo dừa
dishes.desc: Bến Tre's chewy coconut caramels, wrapped in edible rice paper — the Mekong coconut economy in candy form.
dishes.img_query: keo dua ben tre coconut candy
dishes.history:
Kẹo dừa is Bến Tre province — 'the land of coconuts' — reduced to its essence: coconut cream and malt syrup boiled in great woks, poured, cut and hand-wrapped in edible rice paper (the inner wrapper that confuses every first-timer) at a speed that workshop tours present as spectator sport. The candy trade grew from the 1930s into the delta's signature souvenir industry.

Variants track the orchard: durian kẹo dừa for the brave, peanut-studded, pandan. The candy's stubborn chew — it fights back, warms, then floods coconut — is the point; softer imitations exist and Bến Tre ignores them.

dishes.name: Cà phê sữa đá
dishes.desc: Strong robusta drip over sweetened condensed milk and ice; the default.
dishes.history:
The French planted coffee; the highlands made Vietnam the world's largest robusta producer; scarce fresh milk made condensed milk the partner. The phin filter's slow drip built a national ritual of sitting and watching time pass — cà phê sữa đá is less a drink than a pace of life.

The French planted coffee in Vietnam in the 1850s, and the highlands around [Buôn Ma Thuột](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bu%C3%B4n_Ma_Thu%E1%BB%99t) turned out to be a robusta paradise — Vietnam is today the world's largest robusta producer, a fact that shapes the cup: robusta is bitter, brutal and twice the caffeine of arabica, which is why it needs condensed milk, the shelf-stable dairy of a country that historically had no fresh-milk chain.

The phin — the little metal drip filter that sits on the glass — sets the tempo: four to five minutes of watching it drip, which is not dead time but the actual product. Cafe culture in Vietnam is the right to occupy a chair indefinitely; the coffee is the ticket, the sidewalk is the theater. 'Cà phê' is the standard verb for meeting anyone, for any purpose.

The vocabulary is a small syllabus: đen đá (black, ice), sữa đá (condensed milk, ice), sữa nóng for the northern winter; in Hanoi the same drink answers to 'nâu' (brown). The 2010s chain wars (Highlands, Trung Nguyên, Cộng's retro-communist styling) industrialized the format without killing the stool-on-pavement original — both economies run side by side on every block.

dishes.name: Cà phê trứng
dishes.desc: Hanoi invention: coffee under whipped sweet egg-yolk cream, like drinkable tiramisu.
dishes.history:
Invented in 1946 Hanoi by Nguyễn Văn Giảng, a Metropole hotel bartender, when milk was scarce: whipped egg yolk and condensed milk standing in for cream. His family's Café Giảng still serves it; the drink is now a Hanoi heritage item with imitators worldwide.

Egg coffee is a shortage story with a luxurious ending. In 1946 Hanoi, milk was scarce and expensive; Nguyễn Văn Giảng, a bartender at the French-run [Sofitel Metropole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hotel_Metropole_Hanoi) hotel, whipped egg yolk with sugar and condensed milk into a substitute foam, inspired — the family story goes — by cappuccino. He soon left to open Café Giảng, and the drink outlived the shortage that created it.

The construction is precise: robusta brewed strong, the yolk-cream beaten to a meringue-like cloud, the cup often served sitting in a bowl of hot water to keep the layers warm. Done right it drinks like liquid tiramisu with a bitter spine; done lazily it's eggnog on coffee, which is why Hanoians are loyal to specific houses — Giảng (run by the founder's children on Nguyễn Hữu Huân street), Đinh (his daughter's balcony cafe over Hoàn Kiếm lake), each with partisans.

The drink became Hanoi's signature tourist ritual in the 2010s and spawned a family: egg cacao, egg matcha, even egg beer. Da Nang and Saigon cafes serve it now, but the drink remains stubbornly Hanoian — engineered for a northern winter where you hold the cup as much as drink it.

dishes.name: Bạc xỉu
dishes.desc: Condensed-milk-heavy, coffee-light iced drink; the gentler sibling of cà phê sữa.
dishes.history:
From the Cantonese cafés of Chợ Lớn: 'bạc xỉu' abbreviates a phrase meaning 'white with a little coffee', invented for women and children who found cà phê sữa too fierce. It outlived the cafés that named it and is now the gentle default of the southern coffee menu.

Bạc xỉu is Chợ Lớn linguistics you can drink: the name clips the Cantonese phrase 'bạc tẩy xỉu phé' — white cup, little coffee — from the Chinese-run cafes of old Saigon, where the quán cà phê 'dì' (aunties) served clerks, children and women who wanted the ritual without robusta's punch. It's mostly milk (condensed, plus hot water or fresh milk) stained with just enough coffee to justify the glass.

Its 21st-century second act is remarkable: the chains rediscovered the word, and bạc xỉu — iced, often with a salted-cream or coconut variant — became one of the best-selling drinks at Highlands and its competitors, a nostalgic name selling a gentle drink to a generation that never saw the original cafes. Grandparents find this hilarious.

Socially it fills the same slot as a flat white or a milky chai: the non-committal order, the drink for people who came for the conversation. In the Vietnamese coffee taxonomy it marks one pole of a spectrum — đen đá at the ascetic end, bạc xỉu at the indulgent one, cà phê sữa holding the sensible center.

dishes.name: Cà phê muối
dishes.desc: Iced coffee under lightly salted cream foam; a Huế creation now everywhere.
dishes.history:
Salt coffee began at a small Huế café (Cà Phê Muối, opened 2010) — salted cream foam to round robusta's bitterness. It went viral nationally in the 2020s, becoming Huế's biggest culinary export since bún bò and a case study in how fast Vietnamese drink culture now moves.

Salt coffee has a precise birth certificate: Cà Phê Muối, a small shop opened in 2010 by a young couple on Nguyễn Lương Bằng street in Huế, serving exactly one invention — robusta over a fermented-salted cream foam. The salt does what salt does in caramel: mutes bitterness, amplifies sweetness, rounds the robusta's edges. Huế, a city that already salts its lemonade (chanh muối), was the right laboratory.

For a decade it stayed a Huế curiosity; then around 2022–2023 it went algorithmically viral — TikTok queues outside the original shop, salt-coffee menus appearing in Saigon, Hanoi, then Vietnamese cafes abroad, then non-Vietnamese cafes hunting the next dalgona. The original shop still sells it for pocket change, slightly bemused by its own franchise-free fame.

It's the neatest possible case study in how Vietnamese drink culture now iterates: a two-person shop invents, a decade incubates, an app detonates. Order it 'ít ngọt' (less sweet) the first time — the classic build is dessert-forward — and note the correct technique: stir the foam down through the coffee, don't sip them separately.

dishes.name: Nước mía
dishes.desc: Fresh-pressed sugarcane juice over ice, often with kumquat.
dishes.history:
The sugarcane press is one of Vietnam's oldest street machines — cane fed through rollers, juice over ice, kumquat squeezed in against the sweetness. Carts cluster where thirst does: school gates, markets, bus stations. Practically the national electrolyte.

The sugarcane press is one of the oldest machines on the Vietnamese street — hand-cranked for generations, now motorized, always painted the same industrial green — and nước mía is its single product: cane fed through the rollers two or three times, juice strained over ice, a calamansi or kumquat crushed in against the sweetness. The cart's location logic hasn't changed in a century: school gates, market mouths, bus stations, anywhere thirst concentrates.

The drink is agricultural directness — Vietnam grows sugarcane from the northern midlands to the Mekong, and the juice is the crop unprocessed, faintly grassy, absurdly refreshing. Folk medicine files it under 'cooling'; sports science would file it under electrolytes and glucose; both agree with the verdict of anyone who's ridden a bike through a Vietnamese April.

Regional flourishes exist — nước mía sầu riêng (with durian cream) in the west, versions with pineapple pressed through the rollers, the Saigon add-ons of strawberry or coconut — but the canonical glass costs less than the parking fee of anywhere air-conditioned, which is precisely its cultural function: cold sugar democracy.

dishes.name: Sinh tố
dishes.desc: Blended fruit (mango, avocado, soursop) with condensed milk and ice.
dishes.history:
The blender met the tropics: mango, soursop, avocado and condensed milk made sinh tố the fruit-stall counterpart of café culture. Every neighbourhood has its sinh tố lady, and the genre quietly showcases the Mekong's orchard wealth better than any fruit plate.

Sinh tố is what happened when the blender met one of the world's great fruit baskets. The format — fruit, ice, condensed milk, sometimes yogurt — turned the Mekong's orchards into a drinkable menu: mango, soursop (mãng cầu), sapodilla (sa pô chê), avocado, custard apple, jackfruit, dragonfruit, mixed 'thập cẩm' for the undecided. Every neighborhood has a sinh tố stall with fruit stacked like a market display; you point, they blend.

The condensed-milk backbone ties it to the same no-fresh-dairy history as the coffee: richness had to come from the can, and the slight caramel note of sweetened condensed milk is now simply what Vietnamese smoothies taste like — substituting fresh milk reads as dietetic, not authentic.

Its social slot is the evening: sinh tố stalls fill after dinner, families on stools, teenagers on dates, the stall lady remembering regulars' orders. Foreign smoothie chains keep opening and keep discovering they can't compete with a stall that buys its mangoes at 5am and charges a third the price for twice the fruit.

dishes.name: Trà đá
dishes.desc: Weak iced tea, near-free at every eatery; trà chanh (lime) is the hangout drink.
dishes.history:
Free iced tea with everything is a southern hospitality codified into custom — refusing to charge for trà đá is near-law for rice eateries. Its lime-sweet cousin trà chanh spawned Hanoi's 'trà chanh chém gió' (tea and hot air) youth-corner culture. The cheapest institution in Vietnamese food, and one of the most binding.

Trà đá is the free layer of Vietnamese hospitality: weak tea — often the stems and dust left from proper tea production — brewed in bulk, iced, and set on the table before you've ordered anything, refilled without being asked, priced at nothing or next to it. Southern eateries treat charging for it as a moral failing; the pitcher on the table is as standard as the chopstick jar.

In Hanoi it grew its own institution: the trà đá vỉa hè — sidewalk tea stall — a stool, a kettle, sunflower seeds, and the city's real news exchange. 'Trà đá' in the north is a social verb the way 'cà phê' is nationally; the phrase 'trà chanh chém gió' ('lime tea and slicing the wind', i.e., talking nonsense) canonized the youth version that boomed around Hanoi's cathedral in the 2010s.

Economists have used the unchanging price of a glass of trà đá as a joke inflation index; sociologists point out the stalls are where drivers, students and office workers actually mix. Either way, the drink itself is almost beside the point — trà đá is infrastructure.

variants.name: trà chanh
variants.note: lime tea — the Hanoi youth-corner hangout version

variants.name: trà chanh giã tay
variants.note: hand-pounded whole-lime version, the 2023 viral wave

variants.name: trà đào cam sả
variants.note: the canonical build: peach + orange + bruised lemongrass

variants.name: trà đào đặc biệt
variants.note: 'special' — each shop's branded house mix, usually extra fruit, cream foam or jelly; ask what's in it

variants.name: trà vải
variants.note: lychee instead of peach, same genre

variants.name: trà ổi hồng
variants.note: pink guava version, newer wave

dishes.name: Trà đào
dishes.desc: The fruit-tea flagship: black tea over canned-peach fans, orange and lemongrass — the drink of the 2010s café boom.
dishes.img_query: tra dao cam sa peach tea
dishes.history:
Trà đào is the drink that re-invented the Vietnamese tea shop: strong black tea poured over fanned slices of canned peach, with orange and bruised lemongrass — sweet, perfumed, photogenic, and engineered for the Instagram decade. Chains like Phúc Long (whose bitter-strong tea base built a cult) and a thousand imitators turned 'trà đào cam sả' into the default order of office Vietnam in the 2010s, the tea counterpart of cà phê sữa đá.

The 'đặc biệt' on menus is branding, not taxonomy — every shop's special is its own tweak of fruit, foam or jelly, which is why the decoder can't tell you exactly what arrives; the note on the menu next to the price usually can. The genre keeps mutating (lychee, guava, cheese-foam caps borrowed from Taiwan), but the canned peach fan on the glass's rim remains its flag.

variants.name: trà sen Tây Hồ
variants.note: the traditional treasure: green tea scented inside lotus blooms on West Lake — gift-grade, expensive

variants.name: trà sen kem mặn
variants.note: modern café build: lotus tea under a salted-cream foam cap, cà phê muối's tea cousin

variants.name: trà sen vàng
variants.note: the chain-menu version with lotus seeds spooned in

dishes.name: Trà sen
dishes.desc: Two drinks in one name: Hanoi's laborious lotus-scented tea of West Lake, and the modern café lotus drinks with salted-cream caps.
dishes.history:
Traditional trà sen is Hanoi's most extravagant act of patience: green tea sealed overnight inside living lotus blossoms on West Lake, or layered with hand-separated lotus 'rice' (the anthers) — folklore prices a kilo of the true Tây Hồ article in the thousands of blooms, and it's gifted the way rare whisky is gifted elsewhere. Scholars' tea, ancestors' tea, the scent of a Hanoi summer distilled.

The café generation kept the perfume and dropped the ceremony: lotus tea now anchors chain menus with lotus seeds in the glass (trà sen vàng made it famous) and, in the newest wave, a salted-cream foam cap — kem mặn — that does for tea what cà phê muối did for coffee. The two lives of the name rarely meet, but both trade on the same idea: the lotus as Vietnam's flower of calm.

dishes.name: Trà gừng
dishes.desc: Hot ginger tea — the rainy-day, sore-throat, upset-stomach prescription that lives on every café and home menu.
dishes.history:
Trà gừng sits exactly on the line between beverage and medicine: fresh ginger simmered or steeped (with or without actual tea leaves, often with honey — trà gừng mật ong), prescribed by every Vietnamese grandmother for colds, rain-soaked commutes, motion sickness and 'trúng gió' — the folk ailment of being 'hit by wind'. Its authority is thermal: ginger is the warming pole of the hot/cool food system that nước sâm and rau má cool down.

Cafés keep it as the rainy-season and post-flu order, Huế pairs it with its sweets, and the candied-ginger (mứt gừng) tradition at Tết is the same root in solid form. One of the few drinks ordered for how you feel rather than how it tastes — though on a wet Da Nang December night, it also just tastes right.

dishes.name: Trà atisô
dishes.desc: Đà Lạt's artichoke tea — earthy-sweet herbal brew from the French-introduced crop, drunk for the liver and the heat.
dishes.img_query: tra atiso da lat artichoke tea
dishes.history:
The French planted artichokes in Đà Lạt's cool highlands for their tables; the Vietnamese kept the plant and brewed the parts the French discarded — leaves, stems, roots and flower — into trà atisô, an earthy, naturally sweet herbal tea the folk pharmacopeia assigns to the liver: drunk to 'cool' the body after alcohol, fried food and hot weather. The Vietnamized French name (atisô) wears its colonial paper trail openly.

Đà Lạt's market sells it dried, bagged and as the jet-black concentrated cao atisô paste; nationwide it pours from the same roadside jug stands as nước sâm and fills bottled-tea shelves. Café menus list it hot or iced — the rare 'healthy' order that predates the wellness industry by half a century.

variants.name: trà tắc xí muội
variants.note: with salted preserved plum muddled in — sweet-sour-salty, the upgrade order

dishes.name: Trà tắc
dishes.desc: Iced jasmine tea with squeezed kumquats — the pocket-change street cooler sold from every drink cart.
dishes.history:
Trà tắc is the street's minimum viable refreshment: jasmine-scented tea, ice, and little tắc kumquats squeezed in whole, sold from carts and cửa hàng windows for less than any bottled drink. Saigon's school gates and office alleys run on it; the xí muội upgrade — a salted plum muddled into the glass — adds the sweet-salty dimension Vietnamese drinks love (see chanh muối).

It shares the citrus-tea slot with the north's trà chanh (lime tea, covered under trà đá's hangout culture), and the modern chains sell polished versions in sealed cups — but the genre's soul remains a plastic cup, a rubber band, and a vendor who squeezed the kumquats without looking. The gateway drink of the entire Vietnamese tea-street economy.

dishes.name: Ô mai
dishes.desc: Hanoi's preserved-fruit universe: plums and apricots dried, salted, sugared, gingered — sour-sweet-salty pebbles by the lacquered box.
dishes.history:
Ô mai is fruit put through rhetoric school: plums, apricots, kumquats and dracontomelon cured in cycles of salt, sugar, licorice and ginger until each pebble argues sour, sweet, salty and spicy at once. Hanoi's Hàng Đường — 'Sugar Street' — has sold it for generations from shophouses whose lacquered jars are part of the old quarter's furniture; Hồng Lam and its rivals turned the craft into a gift industry.

Culturally it's the taste of the Hanoi handbag: an auntie's pocket candy, the thing pressed on visitors with tea, traditional medicine's throat remedy (ginger-plum for coughs), and the souvenir every southern relative expects. The name likely derives from a Chinese preserved-plum tradition (wumei), but the flavor grammar — that four-way argument — is thoroughly Hanoian.

dishes.name: Nước sâm
dishes.desc: Chilled sweet herbal tea from roadside jugs, meant to counter the heat.
dishes.history:
Cooling herbal teas from the Chinese medicinal tradition, poured from roadside glass jugs in the south: sugarcane, pandan, corn silk, monk fruit. Folk thermal logic — the body runs 'hot', sâm cools it — keeps the jugs full through the long southern summer.

Nước sâm is Chợ Lớn's herbal-tea tradition gone to the streets: the Cantonese cooling-tea (leung cha) counter translated into Vietnamese as glass jugs of amber 'sâm' — despite the name, no ginseng involved — brewed from sugarcane, pandan, corn silk, water chestnut, monk fruit (la hán quả), or the 24-herb bitter blends for the committed. Southern heat plus Chinese humoral medicine equals a beverage category.

The operating theory is 'mát' — cooling — the folk-physiological idea that fried food, late nights and hot weather make the body 'nóng' (hot: breakouts, mouth ulcers, restlessness), and that the right decoction rebalances it. Whether or not the physiology convinces you, the fridge-cold monk-fruit tea after a fried lunch makes an empirical argument.

The jugs line pharmacy-adjacent stalls in District 5 and 6, night markets, and increasingly bottled-drink fridges nationwide — industry noticed and now sells canned trà bí đao (winter melon) and sâm blends. The homemade jug at a street stall, though, remains the real thing: recipe from a grandmother, clientele from the whole block.

dishes.name: Bia hơi
dishes.desc: Fresh unpasteurized draft beer, brewed daily, sold for pocket change on plastic stools. An institution, not a beverage.
dishes.history:
Bia hơi is beer with a daily expiry and a social contract: light lager (rice in the grain bill, ~4%), unpasteurized, delivered each morning in steel kegs and gone by night. The Czech-influenced brewing arrived via socialist-era exchange, and the ritual formed around scarcity — queues with jerry cans in the subsidy years — then relaxed into the plastic-stool corner democracy Hanoi is famous for: glasses of it cost less than parking, and 'bia hơi Hà Nội' corners (Tạ Hiện's international crossroads most visibly) function as the city's evening living room.

The glass itself is heritage: the thick, bubbled, hand-blown green tumbler — cheap enough to lose, iconic enough to be collected. The toast is 'một hai ba, dô!' and the food orbit (đậu phụ mắm tôm, dồi sụn, peanuts) is its own cuisine. Da Nang and the south run their own draft cultures, but the archetype is a Hanoi pavement at 5pm.

dishes.name: Sữa đậu nành
dishes.desc: Fresh soy milk — hot in glass bottles at dawn, iced with pandan by night. The street's gentlest drink.
dishes.history:
Fresh soy milk is the street's morning dairy: beans ground and boiled before dawn, sold hot in recycled glass bottles at markets and school gates, often perfumed with pandan leaf. It predates and outlives every packaged version — the powdered and carton brands exist precisely because the street set the habit.

Đà Lạt gave it a second shift: the night-market ritual of hot sữa đậu nành with bánh ngọt against the highland chill. Elsewhere it's the drink of students, monks and anyone the coffee would rattle — and the classic pairing with bánh tai in Phú Thọ or quẩy anywhere shows its real job: the neutral, warm partner to everything fried.

variants.name: rau má đậu xanh
variants.note: blended with mung bean and coconut milk — creamy, more dessert than drink

dishes.name: Rau má
dishes.desc: Grass-green pennywort juice, blended fresh over ice — vegetal, cooling, the chlorophyll answer to the heat.
dishes.history:
Rau má is lawn-colored medicine that became a soft drink: pennywort (Centella asiatica, gotu kola to the wellness industry) blended raw with water and sugar into a green glass that tastes like cucumber's herbal cousin. Folk physiology classes it as deeply 'mát' — cooling — prescribed for heat rash, late nights and fried-food remorse; modern skincare marketing rediscovered the same leaf as 'cica'.

The juice-stall version over ice is the southern classic; the upgrade, rau má đậu xanh with mung bean and coconut, turned it into a creamy genre of its own with dedicated chains. One of the few street drinks that's arguably good for you and tastes like it — in the best way.

dishes.name: Chanh muối
dishes.desc: Limes preserved in salt for months, muddled into sweet-salty limeade or soda — the electrolyte drink before the word existed.
dishes.history:
Chanh muối starts in glass jars on sunny windowsills: whole limes packed in salt and pressed for months until they turn translucent amber and mellow into something between citrus and pickle. Muddled with sugar and water or soda, the preserved fruit makes the drink — sweet, salty, deeply sour, and quietly the smartest thing to drink after sweating through a Vietnamese noon (salt plus sugar plus fluid: oral rehydration, folk edition).

Every drink stall keeps a jar, grandmothers gift them, and the pantry logic extends to a whole preserved-citrus family (tắc muối kumquats, mơ muối apricots) that doubles as cough medicine. Huế's salt-leaning palate — see cà phê muối — makes particular sense of it.

dishes.name: Soda hột gà
dishes.desc: Raw egg yolk whipped with condensed milk, hit with a jet of soda — frothy, rich, fizzy; the Chợ Lớn tonic-café classic.
dishes.history:
Soda sữa hột gà is the drink equivalent of a dare that turns out delicious: an egg yolk beaten glossy with condensed milk in the bottom of a tall glass, then 'cooked' by a violent stream of soda water poured from height, foaming into something between cream soda and liquid custard. It belongs to the old Chinese-Vietnamese café tradition of Chợ Lớn — the same tonic logic as sâm bổ lượng: eggs for strength, served cold for the climate.

Parents ordered it for skinny children for generations, which gives it a nostalgia halo among Saigonese of a certain age. It's egg coffee's southern cousin by chemistry — yolk plus condensed milk — minus the coffee, plus the fizz, and made twenty years earlier by cafés that measured wellness in yolks.

dishes.name: Rượu cần
dishes.desc: Highland jar wine: fermented rice and husks drunk communally through long bamboo straws stuck in the crock.
dishes.history:
Rượu cần is drinking as architecture: glutinous rice (or cassava) fermented with forest-herb yeast cakes in a tall clay jar under a cap of husks, opened by pouring in spring water and planting long bamboo straws — cần — through which the circle drinks together, host first, honored guest next. The jar is the table, the ritual and the measure: gourds of water refill it, and etiquette scores how much you lower the level on your turn.

It belongs to the highland peoples — Ê Đê, Ba Na, Gia Rai, Mường, Thái — where no festival, wedding or buffalo ceremony proceeds without jars lashed to the house posts. Lowland tourism borrows it for gong-show evenings around Buôn Ma Thuột and Kon Tum; the real thing, opened for a village occasion, remains one of Southeast Asia's great communal drinking technologies — sweet-sour, cloudy, stronger than it tastes.

dishes.name: Rượu đế
dishes.lit: 'sedge-grass wine' — hidden from tax men in the grass
dishes.desc: Village rice moonshine, clear and strong (35–45%), distilled from fermented rice — the spirit under every folk toast.
dishes.history:
Rượu đế is Vietnam's moonshine with a colonial grudge in its name: when the French monopolized alcohol production, village distillers hid their stills and jars in the đế sedge grass — the northern nickname 'quốc lủi' ('national sneak') tells the same story. Rice or sticky rice, yeast cake, one rustic still: the result is a clear 35–45% spirit that smells of banana esters and burns with agricultural honesty.

It's the liquor of đám giỗ death anniversaries, wedding toasts and nhậu sessions in every province — drunk in small cups, poured in rounds, refused at social cost. Famous village appellations (Bàu Đá in Bình Định, Gò Đen near Saigon, Kim Sơn in the north) function like folk crus. Quality varies from sublime to hazardous; the standing advice is to drink where the family distills.

dishes.name: Rượu thuốc
dishes.desc: Rice spirit infusing jars of herbs, roots, fruits — or fauna — into folk tonics; every countryside cabinet has one.
dishes.history:
Rượu thuốc is the pharmacy annex of the rượu world: rượu đế poured over medicinal payloads — ginseng and goji at the respectable end, apricots and mulberries at the delicious end, and a menagerie (seahorses, geckos, silkworms, the notorious snakes) at the theatrical end — then aged months into amber tonics assigned to ailments: aches, digestion, 'strength', and above all male vigor, the folk category that sells the most jars.

The glass jars glowing gold and umber along a countryside restaurant's wall are part décor, part dispensary; a host pouring you a thimble of his ten-year chuối hột (wild banana) wine is offering both hospitality and a health opinion. Efficacy is folkloric; the ritual — one small cup, savored, discussed — is real medicine for the table's mood.

dishes.name: Rượu rắn
dishes.desc: Rice spirit with a whole snake coiled in the jar — the most photographed member of the medicine-wine family.
dishes.history:
Rượu rắn is rượu thuốc's most infamous branch: a cobra or banded krait coiled photogenically in a jar of rice spirit, sometimes with scorpions or ginseng as supporting cast, sold on the folk theory that the snake's essence transfers courage and virility to the drinker. The tradition is genuinely old — snake villages like Lệ Mật near Hanoi built whole economies on serpent cuisine and wine — but the jar in a tourist-strip window is mostly performance: the alcohol does the preserving, the snake does the marketing.

Decoder-grade honesty: much of what's sold to visitors is a souvenir with wildlife-protection problems (some species are protected; many jars are illegal to export or import — customs seizes them routinely), and the health claims are folklore. Documented here because you'll see it, photograph it, and be offered a shot at some countryside table — one thimble for the story is the traditional dose.
